speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: Students have filled the streets over the civil-service quota system — peaceful, enormous, and growing by the hour. The IGP wants permission to clear the intersections before they become permanent. "Give it a week," he warns, "and a road-block becomes a movement."

left.label: Authorise a forceful clearance now.
left.tooltip: A crowd unanswered only multiplies.
left.reveal: The batons and gas clear the roads for a day. The footage of bloodied students travels the world and triples the next crowd.

right.label: Hold the police back, open negotiations.
right.tooltip: You cannot tear-gas a generation into loving you.
right.reveal: You meet the student leaders. The cantonment frets that you flinched; the campuses, astonished, lower the temperature.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: Despite restraint, a video of a single unarmed student shot dead has become the symbol of the movement. The protest is no longer about quotas — it is about you. Your hardliners demand a curfew and an internet blackout.

left.label: Impose curfew and cut the internet.
left.tooltip: Silence the signal, scatter the crowd.
left.reveal: The blackout buys an eerie quiet and global condemnation. Businesses lose crores a day and the anger compounds in the dark.

right.label: Keep the internet on, hold an inquiry into the killing.
right.tooltip: A government that hides has already lost.
right.reveal: You name an inquiry and let the country watch. It is a gamble on transparency; the movement, given a hearing, begins to fracture between moderates and absolutists.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: The movement now demands the resignation of the ministers who ordered the violence — and the cantonment chief privately signals he will not order troops to fire on students again. The ground is shifting under your chair.

left.label: Sacrifice the hardline ministers.
left.tooltip: Better to lose a wing than the whole bird.
left.reveal: You dismiss the ministers blamed for the deaths. The crowd claims a scalp and pauses; your inner circle learns no one is safe.

right.label: Stand by your ministers, dig in.
right.tooltip: I will not feed my own people to a mob.
right.reveal: You refuse to bend and rally the loyal core. But with the army unwilling to shoot, your authority now rests on a crowd's patience.

speaker: Rights Commission Chair
prompt: The Commission hands you a dossier: dozens vanished into the custody of your elite battalion, families turned away at every gate. "Some are alive in secret cells," the Chair says quietly. "You could bring them home. Or you could let the file gather dust like the last one."

left.label: Bury the dossier.
left.tooltip: The battalion is the spine of internal order.
left.reveal: The file vanishes and the cells stay full. The disappeared remain disappeared, and the rot spreads through the ranks that learn it is permitted.

right.label: Order the secret detainees released and investigated.
right.tooltip: A state that disappears its own has already fallen.
right.reveal: Survivors come home to weeping families and cameras. The battalion's commanders feel the betrayal; the world cautiously applauds.

speaker: Rights Commission Chair
prompt: With the cells emptying, a faction of officers — afraid of prosecution — floats a quiet warning through intermediaries: 'protect us with amnesty, or the force cannot guarantee its loyalty.' It is a polite mutiny.

left.label: Grant a quiet amnesty for past acts.
left.tooltip: Loyalty now outweighs justice later.
left.reveal: The officers stand down, reassured. Impunity is enshrined, and the families of the disappeared learn there will be no reckoning.

right.label: Refuse amnesty, reform the command instead.
right.tooltip: I will not be blackmailed by my own guns.
right.reveal: You retire the worst commanders and split the battalion's powers. It is dangerous — a wounded force is unpredictable — but the precedent of accountability holds.

speaker: ICT Enforcement Director
prompt: Under the cyber law, a cartoonist and two reporters sit in jail for posts mocking the first family. The Director offers more arrests under the same statute — "a few examples and the rest fall quiet" — or you could let the three out and blunt the law's teeth.

left.label: Make more examples under the Act.
left.tooltip: Mockery unchecked becomes contempt.
left.reveal: The arrests chill the newsrooms into caution. Editors self-censor, and 'press freedom' becomes the West's favourite stick to beat you with.

right.label: Free the three and amend the worst clauses.
right.tooltip: A government afraid of cartoons is afraid of itself.
right.reveal: The journalists walk free to grateful coverage. Your thin-skinned loyalists fume; your standing with the open world recovers.

speaker: District Magistrate, riot zone
prompt: During the Durga Puja, a rumour of a desecrated Quran has set mobs loose on Hindu temples and homes across several districts. The local police 'watched'. The Magistrate begs for orders before nightfall, when it will spread.

left.label: Deploy decisively and arrest the instigators.
left.tooltip: A pogrom on my watch is my disgrace.
left.reveal: Troops restore order overnight and the ringleaders are jailed. The hardline preachers who fanned it turn their sermons against you.

right.label: Issue a balanced statement, avoid 'taking sides'.
right.tooltip: Do not inflame the majority before an election.
right.reveal: You stay above it and let it burn out. The minorities learn their safety is negotiable; the cynics call it electoral arithmetic.

speaker: Cyber Intelligence Vendor
prompt: A foreign vendor offers a phone-hacking suite that can crack any device in the country — opposition, press, your own ministers. "Every modern state has one," the salesman murmurs. The price is steep and the oversight is whatever you decide it is.

left.label: Buy it and use it freely.
left.tooltip: Knowledge of every plot is worth the cost.
left.reveal: You gain a god's-eye view of your enemies — and your allies. When its existence leaks, as such things do, the scandal is radioactive.

right.label: Decline, or buy it under judicial warrant only.
right.tooltip: A tool with no leash is a leash on me.
right.reveal: You either pass or wrap it in court oversight. You lose some omniscience; you keep a state that can still call itself lawful.

speaker: Reform-minded Police Superintendent
prompt: A young SP requests an audience after a hawker dies in his thana's lock-up. "My OC tortured him over forty taka and wrote it as a heart attack. I can sign the false report and rise, or report my own station and be buried. I came to you to decide which."

left.label: Tell him to sign and let it lie.
left.tooltip: One hawker is not worth a war in the ranks.
left.reveal: The report is falsified and the SP, complicit now, climbs. Every constable learns the lock-up has no witnesses that matter.

right.label: Back him, prosecute the OC, protect the SP.
right.tooltip: A force that tortures the poor protects no one.
right.reveal: You make the honest officer a symbol and the OC an example. The old guard closes ranks against you; the public, briefly, believes in the police.

speaker: Camp Security Commander
prompt: The refugee mega-camps have become a black economy of yaba, trafficking, and rival armed gangs. The commander wants a hard 'cleansing' operation; the aid agencies warn it will be a bloodbath that the satellites will see.

left.label: Authorise the hard operation.
left.tooltip: A lawless camp threatens the whole coast.
left.reveal: The gangs are crushed and the camp goes quiet. Civilian casualties and footage of the sweep hand your critics a year of ammunition.

right.label: Build a vetted camp police with the agencies.
right.tooltip: Order earned beats order imposed.
right.reveal: You stand up a joint, accountable force. It is slow and imperfect, but the worst gangs lose their grip without a massacre.

speaker: Special Branch Director
prompt: With the vote months away, the Special Branch proposes a sweep: mass cases against opposition organisers to 'thin their machine' before the campaign. "Empty their streets now," the Director says, "and the result handles itself."

left.label: Greenlight the pre-election sweep.
left.tooltip: A contest you control is a contest you win.
left.reveal: Thousands of organisers are jailed on old cases. The opposition cries foul to a sympathetic world; the result is yours and tainted.

right.label: Forbid it, let them campaign.
right.tooltip: A win that needs empty jails is a loss in disguise.
right.reveal: You let the opposition march. The contest is real and dangerous; if you win, you win something worth holding.

speaker: Anti-Narcotics Director
prompt: Methamphetamine pours over the eastern border and floods the slums. The director proposes a hardline 'war on drugs' — sweeping arrests and a quiet tolerance for crossfire deaths of suspected dealers.

left.label: Launch the war on drugs.
left.tooltip: Burn the trade out, root and branch.
left.reveal: The headlines blaze with seizures. The 'crossfire' bodies pile up, many of them poor and unproven, and the rights world recoils.

right.label: Treat addiction as health, target the kingpins.
right.tooltip: You cannot shoot your way out of a chemical.
right.reveal: You build clinics and chase the financiers instead. It is slow and unglamorous; the slums, in time, breathe easier.

speaker: Road Safety Movement
prompt: Two students are crushed by a reckless bus and the city's schoolchildren pour into the streets, checking licences and shaming the state's rotten road system. The traffic — and your authority — is paralysed.

left.label: Concede: pass a real road-safety law.
left.tooltip: The children are policing what we would not.
left.reveal: You meet their demands and jail the worst operators. The transport-owner lobby, thick with your men, is enraged.

right.label: Clear the streets and brand it a plot.
right.tooltip: Schoolchildren do not run my roads.
right.reveal: Your cadres and police break the movement. The images of beaten students travel the world and stain the year.

speaker: District Police Super
prompt: A WhatsApp rumour of child-snatchers has set off a wave of mob lynchings; innocent travellers are being beaten to death in village squares. The SP wants emergency powers and an internet throttle.

left.label: Throttle the rumour networks and flood police out.
left.tooltip: Silence the lie, save the lives.
left.reveal: The lynchings ebb as connectivity drops and patrols rise. Businesses and rights groups protest the heavy throttle.

right.label: Run a public-truth campaign, no shutdown.
right.tooltip: You fight a rumour with the truth, not a blackout.
right.reveal: You blanket the airwaves with the facts and arrest ringleaders. Slower to bite, but the open net is preserved.

speaker: Hill Tracts Commander
prompt: The decades-old Hill Tracts accord is fraying; an armed indigenous faction ambushes a patrol while Bengali settlers demand protection and more land. The commander wants a hard sweep of the hills.

left.label: Order the military sweep.
left.tooltip: Restore the writ of the state in the hills.
left.reveal: The faction scatters under pressure. Indigenous villages report abuses, settlers push deeper, and the grievance hardens.

right.label: Revive the accord and freeze settlement.
right.tooltip: The hills were promised autonomy; honour it.
right.reveal: You restart talks and halt the land grab. The settler lobby and some generals seethe; the hills, warily, quiet.

speaker: Women's Rights Commission
prompt: A horrific assault, filmed and shared, has the country marching for justice and the death penalty for rape. The commission wants real reform — fast courts, victim protection — not just a hanging law.

left.label: Pass capital punishment and call it done.
left.tooltip: Give the street the sentence it demands.
left.reveal: The crowd is sated by the gallows clause. Conviction rates barely move; the deeper rot in policing and courts is untouched.

right.label: Build fast-track courts and victim protection.
right.tooltip: A noose is not a justice system.
right.reveal: You fund the slow machinery of real protection. Less cathartic, but conviction and reporting finally begin to climb.

speaker: Anti-Trafficking Chief
prompt: Smugglers sell desperate young men a deadly dream — boats to Europe via Libya, where many drown or are ransomed. The trade runs through brokers in your own districts who pay local police.

left.label: Smash the broker networks and the dirty police.
left.tooltip: We bury our youth in the Mediterranean.
left.reveal: You jail brokers and the officers on their payroll. The trade fragments; the bought policemen and their patrons turn on you.

right.label: Look away; the remittances and the relief valve matter.
right.tooltip: Desperate men will go regardless.
right.reveal: The boats keep sailing and the brokers keep paying. Every drowned son's family learns the state shrugged.

speaker: Prisons Inspector-General
prompt: The jails hold three times their capacity, many of them opposition men on old cases never tried. The inspector warns of a tuberculosis outbreak and a riot brewing in the central jail.

left.label: Release the untried on bail to ease the jails.
left.tooltip: A prison of the un-charged is a scandal.
left.reveal: Thousands walk free pending trial; the jails decompress. Your hardliners call it surrender to the opposition.

right.label: Hold the line; build more cells.
right.tooltip: The streets are safer with them inside.
right.reveal: You keep the jails full and pour money into more bars. The outbreak spreads, and the world counts your political prisoners.

speaker: Fire Service Director
prompt: A chemical warehouse in a packed old-city lane ignites and the death toll climbs past a hundred. The director wants every illegal warehouse and unsafe high-rise shut — owned, mostly, by the connected.

left.label: Shut the death-trap buildings citywide.
left.tooltip: No more pyres in the old city.
left.reveal: Inspectors padlock hundreds of illegal premises. Commerce screams and powerful landlords mobilise against the crackdown.

right.label: Fine them and let them reopen.
right.tooltip: You cannot empty half the old city.
right.reveal: A few fines, a press conference, and the lanes reopen as before. The next fire is already being stacked, drum by drum.

speaker: Immigration Director
prompt: Getting a passport means paying a broker who splits the bribe with your own officers; the honest applicant waits months. The director offers a fully digital, broker-proof system — and a revolt from the staff who feed on the racket.

left.label: Force the digital, broker-proof rollout.
left.tooltip: A passport is a right, not a bribe.
left.reveal: The brokers lose their grip and applicants their agony. The displaced officers sabotage and slander the new system.

right.label: Keep the human counters and the side-economy.
right.tooltip: Do not provoke the whole department at once.
right.reveal: The counters stay, and so does the toll on every citizen. The racket hums on under your seal.

speaker: VIP Protocol Officer
prompt: A minister's speeding convoy killed a rickshaw-puller and an ambulance idled behind the cavalcade as a patient died. The footage is everywhere; the protocol officer asks how to handle the minister.

left.label: Discipline the minister, curb VIP convoys.
left.tooltip: No siren outruns an ambulance.
left.reveal: You publicly clip the privilege and the minister. The cabinet's grandees fume at the humiliation; the street cheers.

right.label: Protect the minister, manage the press.
right.tooltip: One does not feed a colleague to a mob.
right.reveal: The story is smothered and the convoys roll on. The dead man's family, and the watching city, file it away.

speaker: NID Database Chief
prompt: The national ID database — fingerprints, faces, the whole population — could be wired to a live surveillance grid. The chief says it would find any dissident in minutes. It would also find anyone, anytime.

left.label: Build the live surveillance grid.
left.tooltip: A state that sees all fears nothing.
left.reveal: You gain a near-omniscient eye over the country. When its reach leaks, the outrage at home and abroad is volcanic.

right.label: Wall the database behind strict warrants.
right.tooltip: A tool that watches everyone is a chain on everyone.
right.reveal: You keep the ID system civil, not panoptic. You lose an instrument of control and keep a shred of liberty.

speaker: Cyber Crime Unit Head
prompt: Online betting rings and OTP-fraud gangs are fleecing the public through foreign-hosted sites, and some local agents are tied to party youth. The head wants a sweeping cyber-crackdown.

left.label: Crack down hard, agents and all.
left.tooltip: Cut the scam off at the local hand.
left.reveal: Arrests roll up the fraud rings and the party-linked agents both. A slice of your youth wing is suddenly facing court.

right.label: Chase the foreign sites, spare the local agents.
right.tooltip: Do not jail our own boys over a side hustle.
right.reveal: You block sites and spare the agents. The fraud reroutes and the public keeps bleeding small sums into the dark.

speaker: Police Recruitment Board
prompt: Constable jobs are openly sold for lakhs, so the force fills with men in debt to the brokers who placed them — and primed to extort to repay. The board offers a transparent, merit-only recruitment.

left.label: Enforce merit-only recruitment.
left.tooltip: A bought constable polices for his broker.
left.reveal: Honest recruits enter a cleaner force. The recruitment mafias and the local bosses who ran them lose a fortune and turn hostile.

right.label: Keep the comfortable status quo.
right.tooltip: Do not war with every thana at once.
right.reveal: The jobs keep selling and the new constables keep extorting to clear their debts. The rot renews itself.

speaker: Transport Authority Chief
prompt: The capital is one vast jam of unfit, unlicensed vehicles run by syndicates that pay everyone. The chief wants to scrap the death-trap buses — owned by men who can put thousands of strikers on the road.

left.label: Scrap the unfit fleets.
left.tooltip: A city cannot run on rolling coffins.
left.reveal: Dangerous buses come off the road and the air clears a little. The transport syndicates shut the city down in retaliation.

right.label: Negotiate softly with the syndicates.
right.tooltip: They can paralyse the capital in an hour.
right.reveal: You trade enforcement for peace with the bosses. The coffins keep rolling and the gridlock thickens.

speaker: Counter-Terror Chief
prompt: After a café siege shocked the world, the chief has cells under watch and wants pre-emptive raids. Go too hard and you radicalise the pious mainstream; go too soft and the next attack is on you.

left.label: Authorise aggressive pre-emptive raids.
left.tooltip: Strike the cells before they strike us.
left.reveal: The plots are disrupted and the world credits your resolve. Heavy-handed raids on the wrong doors breed quiet new grievances.

right.label: Pair surgical policing with de-radicalisation.
right.tooltip: You cannot raid a sermon out of a mind.
right.reveal: You blend precise arrests with community work. Slower reassurance for the West, but a thinner recruiting ground over time.

speaker: Slum Eviction Magistrate
prompt: A vast riverside slum sits on prime state land a developer covets, and the magistrate has bulldozers ready. Half a million of the city's poorest — your voters — live in those tin lanes.

left.label: Send in the bulldozers for the developer.
left.tooltip: The land is worth more than the shanties.
left.reveal: The towers will rise and the treasury will profit. Half a million are scattered into the monsoon, and they remember your name.

right.label: Halt the eviction, regularise the settlement.
right.tooltip: You do not bulldoze your own people for a tower.
right.reveal: You give the slum legal footing and basic services. The developer and his patrons lose a fortune and your favour.

speaker: Special Branch Analyst
prompt: The branch has assembled a watchlist of journalists, academics, and activists — 'persons of interest'. The analyst wants authority to tap, trail, and pressure them pre-emptively.

left.label: Authorise the surveillance dragnet.
left.tooltip: Know your critics before they move.
left.reveal: You blind-side dissent before it organises. When the watchlist leaks, every name becomes a martyr and a headline.

right.label: Disband the watchlist.
right.tooltip: A state afraid of its scholars is already weak.
right.reveal: You stand down the dragnet. You lose an early-warning net and keep the country a place thought can still be spoken.

speaker: Border Guard Sector Commander
prompt: Cattle, phensedyl, and gold flow across the porous border through villages that live on the trade, while the neighbour's force shoots the smugglers dead. The commander wants legal border-haats to replace the black market.

left.label: Open legal border markets.
left.tooltip: Trade in daylight beats trade under fire.
left.reveal: Sanctioned haats pull commerce into the open and cut the killings. The smuggling syndicates lose their margins and snarl.

right.label: Crack down hard on the border villages.
right.tooltip: Smuggling is smuggling; stamp it out.
right.reveal: Raids choke the trade and the border economy collapses. The villages, robbed of their living, curse the state that 'saved' them.

speaker: Riot Control Commander
prompt: A grand opposition rally has filled the central avenue, peaceful but immense. The commander's finger hovers over the order: disperse it now with gas and batons, or let the sea of people have its day.

left.label: Disperse the rally by force.
left.tooltip: A crowd that size is a threat, not a meeting.
left.reveal: The avenue is cleared in a haze of gas and blood. The footage hardens the opposition and shames you abroad.

right.label: Police it lightly and let it pass.
right.tooltip: Let them shout; tomorrow they go home.
right.reveal: The rally swells, peaks, and disperses on its own. Your hawks call it weakness; the day passes without a martyr.

speaker: Disappearances Liaison
prompt: Mothers of the vanished hold a silent vigil that has become a weekly fixture and a global symbol. A UN body wants to send investigators. Your security men want them refused at the airport.

left.label: Admit the investigators and open the cases.
left.tooltip: A state that hides its vans has lost its soul.
left.reveal: Some of the missing surface; the world cautiously applauds the candour. The agencies responsible feel the ground shift beneath them.

right.label: Refuse the UN team and disperse the mothers.
right.tooltip: No foreigner audits our security.
right.reveal: You bar the investigators and clear the vigil. The mothers' grief becomes the opposition's sharpest, most enduring weapon.

speaker: Domestic Worker Advocate
prompt: A child maid is found tortured in a wealthy home — not the first. The advocate wants registration and inspection of domestic workers, exposing what the comfortable classes do behind their gates.

left.label: Regulate and inspect domestic work.
left.tooltip: No child should vanish behind a rich gate.
left.reveal: Inspections begin to pierce the private cruelty. The affluent — many of them your supporters — resent the intrusion.

right.label: Treat it as a private family matter.
right.tooltip: The state does not enter the drawing room.
right.reveal: You leave the gates closed and the maids unseen. The next tortured child is another headline you will have to manage.

speaker: Election Security Chief
prompt: Polling day looms and rival cadres are arming for the usual battle over the centres. The chief can deploy the army neutrally to guarantee a clean vote — or deploy it where it quietly helps your side.

left.label: Deploy the army strictly neutrally.
left.tooltip: Let the count be clean or not at all.
left.reveal: The vote passes with rare calm and credibility. Your own machine grumbles that you gave away the home advantage.

right.label: Tilt the deployment to your strongholds.
right.tooltip: Security is also strategy.
right.reveal: Your centres are safe; the opposition's are not. You bank the seats and the observers' damning footnotes both.

speaker: Acid & Dowry Cell
prompt: Acid attacks have ticked up again with the price of revenge gone cheap. The cell wants tight control of open acid sales and special tribunals — opposed by the chemical traders' lobby.

left.label: Lock down acid sales, fast-track the cases.
left.tooltip: A face burned for a dowry is a state's failure.
left.reveal: Open acid vanishes from the bazaars and convictions quicken. The chemical traders lobby and grumble, but the wards see fewer victims.

right.label: Leave the trade lightly regulated.
right.tooltip: Do not strangle a whole trade over a few cases.
right.reveal: Acid stays cheap and available. Another disfigured woman, another vigil, another file you will answer for.

speaker: Crowd Safety Officer
prompt: A free-rice distribution and a religious gathering both expect vast crowds the same week, and last year a stampede killed dozens. The officer wants strict crowd-control and fewer, ticketed events.

left.label: Impose strict crowd controls.
left.tooltip: I will not preside over another stampede.
left.reveal: The events run safely under tight management. Some call the controls heavy-handed and the charity less generous.

right.label: Keep the events open and unmanaged.
right.tooltip: Do not fence in the people's gatherings.
right.reveal: The crowds flow free and vast. The risk you waved away is one bad surge from becoming your next tragedy.

speaker: Counter-Terror Commander
prompt: Militants have stormed an upscale café full of foreigners and taken hostages, executing those who cannot recite scripture. The world is watching live. The commander can storm it now with heavy force, or negotiate and wait, each hour risking more throats.

left.label: Storm the café immediately.
left.tooltip: Every hour of delay is another execution.
left.reveal: Commandos breach and end the siege in blood. Some hostages die in the assault, but the militants are wiped out and the state shows it will not be held to ransom.

right.label: Negotiate and wait for a clean rescue.
right.tooltip: A panicked assault fills the morgue.
right.reveal: You hold off and work for a safer resolution. The standoff drags agonisingly and the watching world frets, but a patient rescue may yet save those still alive inside.

speaker: Counter-Terror Commander
prompt: The siege is over, and the attackers turn out to be not impoverished fanatics but privileged, educated young men from good families — a fact that terrifies the elite. The commander wants a sweeping crackdown on suspect youth; the alternative is a quieter de-radicalisation drive.

left.label: Launch a sweeping crackdown on suspect youth.
left.tooltip: Root out the rot before it spreads.
left.reveal: Mass detentions sweep up the young and the bearded. A few cells are broken, but the dragnet radicalises as many as it catches, and the abuses pile up.

right.label: Invest in quiet de-radicalisation.
right.tooltip: You cannot arrest your way out of an idea.
right.reveal: You build counselling, monitoring, and community programmes instead of mass arrests. The results are slow and invisible, but the pipeline of recruits begins, quietly, to thin.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: A rumour on social media that strangers were kidnapping children for a bridge's 'human sacrifice' has triggered a wave of mob lynchings — innocent people beaten to death on suspicion. The IGP wants a hard order to fire on lynch mobs; the alternative is a rumour-control and arrest drive.

left.label: Authorise firing on lynch mobs.
left.tooltip: A mob that kills must fear the gun.
left.reveal: Police open fire to break the worst mobs and the lynchings ebb. The deaths at police hands draw their own outcry, and the underlying panic still smoulders online.

right.label: Crack down on rumours and arrest ringleaders.
right.tooltip: Kill the lie and the mob dissolves.
right.reveal: You flood the networks with truth, arrest instigators, and protect the accused. It is slower than bullets, but the panic deflates without the state adding to the body count.

speaker: Home Secretary
prompt: A vast Islamist movement has occupied the heart of the capital, demanding a blasphemy law and the removal of a 'pagan' sculpture, refusing to leave. The secretary offers a midnight clearance by force, or patient negotiation that concedes some symbolic ground.

left.label: Clear the square by force at night.
left.tooltip: The capital cannot be held hostage.
left.reveal: Police and paramilitaries clear the square in a dark, bloody operation. The occupation ends and rumours of a massacre spread; the movement gains martyrs and a grievance that will not die.

right.label: Negotiate and concede symbolic ground.
right.tooltip: A square emptied by talk leaves no martyrs.
right.reveal: You move the disputed sculpture and promise to 'study' their demands, and the occupation disperses. Secularists feel betrayed by the concession, but the capital is spared a massacre.

speaker: Intelligence Procurement Chief
prompt: A foreign vendor offers military-grade phone spyware that cracks any device — a tool to read every plot, journalist, and judge in the country. The chief is desperate for it; the catch is the inevitable leak that you bought a weapon to spy on your own people.

left.label: Buy the spyware.
left.tooltip: A leader who cannot read the room is blind.
left.reveal: You acquire the spyware and the secrets pour in. Every rival's phone is an open book — until the purchase leaks, and the word 'surveillance state' attaches to your name for good.

right.label: Decline the spyware.
right.tooltip: A tool that reads everyone eventually reads its master's enemies into power.
right.reveal: You pass on the spyware. You forgo a formidable surveillance edge, but you are spared the day an exposé reveals you bugged judges, journalists, and your own ministers.

speaker: Prisons Inspector-General
prompt: The jails hold three times their capacity, swollen with opposition activists swept up in mass cases. Disease and deaths in custody mount. The IG can release the low-level political detainees to ease the crush, or hold them all and build more cells.

left.label: Hold them all, build more cells.
left.tooltip: A freed activist is a refilled street.
left.reveal: You keep the jails packed and order new prisons built. The opposition stays caged and the streets quiet, but the deaths in custody and the cost of the cells both keep climbing.

right.label: Release the low-level political detainees.
right.tooltip: A jail bursting with the innocent shames the state.
right.reveal: You free thousands held on flimsy mass cases. The hardliners warn the streets will fill, but the custodial deaths fall and the courts and jails can breathe.

speaker: University Affairs Officer
prompt: Members of your party's student wing have tortured a student to death in a dorm 'guest room' for a Facebook post criticising the government. The campus and the nation are erupting. The officer asks whether to shield the cadres or hand them to justice.

left.label: Shield the student-wing cadres.
left.tooltip: I do not feed my own loyalists to a mob.
left.reveal: You quietly protect the killers and float counter-narratives. The cover-up enrages the campuses further, and the dead student becomes a permanent symbol of your wing's impunity.

right.label: Hand the killers to justice.
right.tooltip: A boy tortured to death is not a loyalty test.
right.reveal: You let the cadres be arrested and charged. The student wing feels betrayed and the machine grumbles, but the campuses, stunned, see a flicker of accountability.

speaker: Smart City Director
prompt: A 'safe city' project would blanket the capital in cameras with live facial recognition — a genuine boon against crime and terror, and a perfect tool for tracking every protester and dissident. The director wants the full system; civil society is alarmed.

left.label: Deploy the full facial-recognition net.
left.tooltip: A watched city is a safe city.
left.reveal: Cameras and face-tracking blanket the capital. Street crime and terror plots get easier to foil, and so does following every activist from home to rally to home.

right.label: Deploy cameras without facial tracking.
right.tooltip: I will deter the mugger without indexing the marcher.
right.reveal: You install the cameras but bar the face-recognition layer. You gain real crime deterrence without building a tool to map every dissident's movements.

speaker: Security Reform Adviser
prompt: Your elite battalion is under foreign sanctions for extrajudicial killings, and the crossfire deaths have all but stopped under scrutiny — but crime is creeping back and hardliners want the gloves off again. The adviser asks whether to entrench the reform or quietly revert.

left.label: Quietly let the crossfire killings resume.
left.tooltip: Fear was the only law the criminals respected.
left.reveal: You ease the leash and the 'crossfire' deaths quietly return. Crime dips and the hardliners cheer, but the sanctions harden and the reform's brief promise dies.

right.label: Entrench the reform, rebuild on accountability.
right.tooltip: A force that murders cannot be trusted to police.
right.reveal: You lock in the restraint and rebuild the battalion around oversight and proper investigation. Crime-fighting is slower and the hardliners scornful, but the sanctions ease and a killing machine becomes a police force.

speaker: Police Reform Commissioner
prompt: At the local police stations, nothing moves without a bribe — filing a case, getting bail, even reporting a crime. The commissioner wants a transparency overhaul with citizen oversight and digital records; the rank-and-file, who live on the bribes, will resist fiercely.

left.label: Force the transparency overhaul.
left.tooltip: A police that sells justice is a syndicate in uniform.
left.reveal: You impose digital records, citizen oversight, and anti-bribery teams. The rank-and-file seethe and slow-walk, but ordinary people start getting served without paying for it.

right.label: Leave the thana culture alone.
right.tooltip: A demoralised force is a useless one.
right.reveal: You decline to disturb the station-house economy. The constables stay content and pliable, and the poor keep paying a bribe for every scrap of justice the state owes them free.

speaker: Anti-Trafficking Chief
prompt: A trafficking syndicate is loading desperate young men onto death-trap boats for the sea route to Malaysia, where many drown or end in jungle camps — and the network has police and border-guard protection. The chief wants it smashed, names and all.

left.label: Smash the network, protectors and all.
left.tooltip: A trade in drowning men cannot have a uniform's blessing.
left.reveal: You break the syndicate and expose the officers shielding it. The trafficking route is disrupted and the corrupt purged, to international relief and some internal fury.

right.label: Take down only the visible smugglers.
right.tooltip: Some uniforms are better left unexamined.
right.reveal: You arrest the front-line smugglers and leave their protectors untouched. The route reopens under new management within weeks, and the boats keep sailing into the dark.

speaker: Religious Harmony Officer
prompt: A hardline movement is whipping up mobs against a small heterodox Muslim sect, attacking their mosques and demanding they be declared non-Muslim. The officer asks whether to protect the sect with force, or appease the majority by quietly conceding the demand.

left.label: Protect the sect with force.
left.tooltip: The state shields the few from the many, or it shields no one.
left.reveal: You deploy security to defend the sect's mosques and arrest the instigators. The hardline preachers turn their fury on you, but the constitution's promise of equal protection holds.

right.label: Appease the majority, concede the demand.
right.tooltip: I will not burn the country for one small sect.
right.reveal: You quietly distance the state from the sect to placate the mob. The hardliners are triumphant, the violence subsides, and a small community learns the state will trade them for peace.

speaker: Cultural Affairs Officer
prompt: The great secular new-year festival — music, masks, processions — faces a credible militant bomb threat, and hardliners are also denouncing it as 'un-Islamic'. The officer can lock it down behind a security wall, or cancel it 'for safety' and quietly please the zealots.

left.label: Hold the festival behind heavy security.
left.tooltip: Our culture will not be cancelled by a threat.
left.reveal: You ring the festival with security and it goes ahead, defiant and joyous. The cost and the tension are high, but the secular tradition stands and the militants are denied their veto.

right.label: Cancel the festival 'for safety'.
right.tooltip: A dead crowd is too high a price for a parade.
right.reveal: You call it off, citing the threat. The zealots quietly celebrate the cancellation they wanted, the secularists mourn a tradition surrendered, and the militants learn that a threat is enough.

speaker: Internal Affairs Director
prompt: A serving officer has leaked documents proving a senior commander ordered a string of fake 'crossfire' killings. The director can prosecute the whistleblower for breaching secrecy and bury the revelation, or protect him and pursue the commander.

left.label: Prosecute the whistleblower, bury it.
left.tooltip: A leaker is a traitor whatever he reveals.
left.reveal: You jail the whistleblower and quash the story. The killings stay covered, but the lesson lands in every honest officer: speak, and the state will break you, not the killer.

right.label: Protect him, pursue the commander.
right.tooltip: An officer with a conscience is worth ten without.
right.reveal: You shield the whistleblower and move against the commander. The old guard is shaken and warns of low morale, but a culture of impunity takes its first real blow.

speaker: District Magistrate
prompt: The opposition has called a massive rally in the capital, and you can pre-emptively ban all gatherings under the old colonial assembly law, choking it before it starts. The magistrate warns that the ban itself may provoke exactly the confrontation you fear.

left.label: Ban the rally under Section 144.
left.tooltip: A rally forbidden is a rally that cannot grow.
left.reveal: You blanket the city in an assembly ban and flood it with police. The rally is throttled, but the heavy-handed lockdown of the capital becomes the story, and the grievance hardens.

right.label: Permit the rally under conditions.
right.tooltip: A banned gathering becomes a battle; a permitted one becomes a speech.
right.reveal: You allow the rally within agreed limits. The opposition gets its day and its crowd, but the feared confrontation never comes, and the state looks confident rather than afraid.

speaker: Arms Licensing Officer
prompt: Local party leaders are pressing for a wave of private firearms licences — ostensibly for self-defence, in truth to arm the local muscle that wins elections. The officer can approve the lot as a loyalty reward, or tighten licensing and disarm the cadres.

left.label: Approve the firearms licences.
left.tooltip: An armed friend is a loyal friend.
left.reveal: You sign off on the licences and the local strongmen are armed. They deliver elections and intimidate rivals, and the country grows a little more lawless, district by district.

right.label: Tighten licensing, disarm the cadres.
right.tooltip: A party with guns is a militia in waiting.
right.reveal: You clamp down on private firearms, your own side included. The local leaders feel disarmed and betrayed, but the cycle of armed political muscle loosens its grip.

speaker: Counter-Terror Commander
prompt: A handful of citizens who went abroad to fight for a terror group want to return, some with wives and children born in the war zone. The commander can bar or jail them all as a danger, or repatriate and rehabilitate under close watch.

left.label: Bar or jail the returnees outright.
left.tooltip: A trained jihadi is a threat, not a project.
left.reveal: You refuse entry or jail those who slip back. The hard line plays well, but the stranded radicalise further abroad and the children pay for their fathers' choices, drawing rights criticism.

right.label: Repatriate and rehabilitate under watch.
right.tooltip: A child of the caliphate is still a child.
right.reveal: You bring them home into monitoring and de-radicalisation, prosecuting the guilty. It is a risk and the public is uneasy, but you reclaim the salvageable and the innocent rather than abandoning them.

speaker: Information Security Officer
prompt: You can quietly strip parliamentary and secretariat press accreditation from the most critical journalists — cutting their access without an arrest. The officer calls it clean and deniable; it is also an unmistakable signal that coverage is now conditional on loyalty.

left.label: Strip the critics' accreditation.
left.tooltip: Access is a privilege for the friendly.
left.reveal: The critical reporters lose their passes and their scoops. The press corps gets the message and softens, and 'managed media' becomes one more charge in the indictment of your rule.

right.label: Keep accreditation open to all.
right.tooltip: A press corps of flatterers tells me nothing.
right.reveal: You leave access untouched, critics and all. The tough questions keep coming at every briefing, but your government keeps a free press it can point to abroad.

speaker: Prisons Inspector-General
prompt: Convicted militants have broken out of a high-security prison with apparent inside help, and are loose in the country. The IG wants a massive, disruptive nationwide manhunt with checkpoints and detentions; the alternative is a targeted, intelligence-led pursuit.

left.label: Launch a massive nationwide dragnet.
left.tooltip: Flood the country until they surface.
left.reveal: Checkpoints and sweeps blanket the nation. The disruption is enormous and the wrongful detentions many, and the fugitives may still slip the clumsy net.

right.label: Run a targeted intelligence-led pursuit.
right.tooltip: A scalpel catches what a sledgehammer scatters.
right.reveal: You hunt the escapees quietly through intelligence and informants. It is slower and the public nervous, but the country is not paralysed and the inside helpers are traced too.

speaker: Industrial Police Chief
prompt: Tens of thousands of garment workers have walked out over unpaid wages and blocked the industrial highways. The factory owners — your donors — demand the protest crushed; the chief can send the industrial police in hard, or broker a wage settlement instead.

left.label: Crush the worker protest.
left.tooltip: The export zones cannot be held hostage.
left.reveal: The police clear the highways with batons and tear gas, and a worker or two die. The owners are grateful and the lines restart, but the deaths become a global stain on the garment brand.

right.label: Broker a wage settlement.
right.tooltip: Unpaid workers are a fire; do not fan it with batons.
right.reveal: You force the owners to pay the arrears and mediate a raise. The donors grumble at the squeeze, but the highways clear peacefully and the workers go back with their dignity.

speaker: Land Records Commissioner
prompt: Under a notorious old law, minority Hindu families' land has been seized as 'enemy property' and grabbed by the locally powerful — often your own people. A restitution drive could return it; the grabbers, with party ties, will fight to keep their spoils.

left.label: Drive the land restitution.
left.tooltip: Land stolen under a bigot's law must go home.
left.reveal: You push to return the seized property to its rightful owners. The connected grabbers rage and resist parcel by parcel, but a historic injustice begins, at last, to be undone.

right.label: Leave the seized land with its holders.
right.tooltip: I will not evict my own base for old ghosts.
right.reveal: You let the grabbers keep their spoils. Your local power-holders are secured, and the minority families learn once more that the law that robbed them will not give it back.

speaker: NID Database Director
prompt: The national ID system can be linked to phones, banks, travel, and biometrics into a single all-seeing profile of every citizen — efficient government, and a surveillance dossier on the entire population. The director wants full integration; privacy advocates are aghast.

left.label: Build the fully integrated profile.
left.tooltip: A state that knows its citizens governs them well.
left.reveal: You fuse the databases into one omniscient citizen profile. Services get slicker and fraud harder, and the state acquires a dossier on every life that no future ruler will ever dismantle.

right.label: Keep the databases walled off.
right.tooltip: A single key to every life is too much for any hand.
right.reveal: You bar the full integration and keep firewalls between the systems. Government is clunkier and some fraud slips through, but no all-seeing profile of the citizenry is ever assembled.

speaker: Education-Security Liaison
prompt: Tens of thousands of unregistered religious schools teach a curriculum the state cannot see, some shading into extremism. The liaison wants them registered and made to teach maths, science, and citizenship; the clerical establishment calls it an assault on faith.

left.label: Force registration and a modern curriculum.
left.tooltip: A school the state cannot see is a school it cannot trust.
left.reveal: You bring the madrasas under registration and a broadened curriculum. The clerics protest the intrusion and some resist, but a generation gains numeracy and a window onto the wider world.

right.label: Leave the religious schools autonomous.
right.tooltip: I will not pick a fight with every mosque in the land.
right.reveal: You leave the madrasas to themselves to keep the clergy onside. The establishment is grateful, but a vast parallel education stays beyond the state's sight, curriculum and all.

speaker: Traffic Division Chief
prompt: The traffic police live off a daily tax on every bus, truck, and rickshaw, waving through the dangerous and fining the compliant. The chief can launch a clean-up with cashless fines and oversight, or leave the lucrative racket that keeps the force quietly content.

left.label: Clean up the traffic racket.
left.tooltip: A road tax in a constable's pocket is theft with a whistle.
left.reveal: You bring in cashless fines, cameras, and oversight. The constables lose their daily skim and resent it, but the roads grow fractionally fairer and safer.

right.label: Leave the traffic police to their ways.
right.tooltip: A contented constable is a compliant one.
right.reveal: You leave the roadside economy alone. The force stays loyal and well-fed on bribes, and the buses that pay keep killing on the highways while the honest pay the fines.

speaker: Counter-Terror Commander
prompt: Militants are bombing and hacking to death the keepers of Sufi shrines — the gentle, syncretic Islam the hardliners despise. The commander wants visible armed protection at hundreds of shrines and a crackdown on the preachers who incite it; the latter means confronting powerful clerics.

left.label: Protect the shrines and confront the inciters.
left.tooltip: The faith of the saints will not be bombed into silence.
left.reveal: You guard the shrines and move against the preachers fuelling the attacks. The powerful clerics rage at being named, but the pluralist tradition gains a defender and the killings slow.

right.label: Guard the shrines but spare the preachers.
right.tooltip: I will protect the shrine without naming the mosque.
right.reveal: You post guards but avoid confronting the inciting clerics. The attacks continue, fed by sermons you dare not touch, and the shrine-keepers learn that protection stops at the pulpit.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: A horrific gang-rape, filmed and shared by the attackers — politically connected young men — has ignited nationwide protests demanding justice and the death penalty. The IGP asks whether to let the case take its full course against the connected, or let it quietly stall.

left.label: Pursue the connected attackers fully.
left.tooltip: A crime this vile cannot buy its way free.
left.reveal: You let the case proceed hard against the well-connected accused. Their patrons lean on you and lose, the protesters are vindicated, and rare faith in justice flickers back to life.

right.label: Let the case quietly stall.
right.tooltip: Their families are friends I cannot afford to lose.
right.reveal: You let the case sink into delay and lost evidence. The connected walk, the protests curdle into cynicism, and every woman in the country reads the verdict on whose justice this is.

speaker: Border Guard Director
prompt: Your own border guards run a thriving smuggling trade — cattle, drugs, gold, phensedyl — in collusion with their counterparts across the wire. The director can purge the corrupt battalions, risking morale and a guarded border, or keep the lucrative status quo.

left.label: Purge the smuggling battalions.
left.tooltip: A border guard who smuggles guards nothing.
left.reveal: You root out the corrupt commanders and break the rackets. Morale dips and the border briefly wobbles, but the contraband flood eases and the force's discipline begins to mend.

right.label: Leave the border rackets running.
right.tooltip: A purge now leaves the wire unwatched.
right.reveal: You leave the smuggling economy intact to keep the battalions content. The border stays manned and the contraband — drugs especially — keeps pouring across into the towns.

speaker: Home Secretary
prompt: Unrest is spreading city to city and the secretary urges a nationwide curfew with shoot-on-sight authority to 'restore order overnight'. It would clear the streets — and any death under it becomes a death you ordered.

left.label: Impose curfew with shoot-on-sight.
left.tooltip: Order restored at dawn is worth a hard night.
left.reveal: The curfew empties the streets and the unrest is frozen by force. The shoot-on-sight deaths become infamous, and the quiet you buy is the quiet of a country holding its breath in fear.

right.label: Impose a curfew without lethal force.
right.tooltip: A curfew is a pause, not a firing line.
right.reveal: You order a curfew but forbid shooting into crowds, relying on presence and arrests. The streets clear more slowly and unevenly, but the night does not end with a massacre to answer for.

speaker: Telecom Security Officer
prompt: With protests organising by phone, the officer can order a total internet and mobile blackout to blind and scatter them. It would choke the coordination — and the economy, the hospitals, and the country's last window onto what your forces are doing in the dark.

left.label: Order a total communications blackout.
left.tooltip: Cut the signal and the crowd cannot gather.
left.reveal: The country goes dark and the protests fragment. Commerce freezes, hospitals scramble, and the blackout itself — and what it hides — becomes the global headline against you.

right.label: Keep the networks on, target specific cells.
right.tooltip: A nation blinded is a nation that assumes the worst.
right.reveal: You leave the internet up and pursue specific organisers instead. The protests stay coordinated, but the economy runs, the hospitals function, and your forces act in daylight.

speaker: Police Reform Commissioner
prompt: A reform drive proposes body cameras and a real complaints commission for the police — a check on the brutality that fuels public hatred of the force. The rank-and-file and their commanders see it as a leash and a humiliation, and resist hard.

left.label: Mandate body cameras and oversight.
left.tooltip: A camera on the badge is a witness for the citizen.
left.reveal: You force body cameras and an independent complaints body. The commanders bristle and the footage occasionally damns them, but public trust in the police, long dead, stirs faintly.

right.label: Drop the accountability reforms.
right.tooltip: A leashed force is a hesitant one when it matters.
right.reveal: You shelve the cameras and the commission to keep the force happy. The brutality goes on unrecorded, and the gulf between the police and the people they answer to yawns wider.

speaker: District Magistrate
prompt: Self-appointed morality squads are harassing couples in parks, attacking 'immodest' women, and beating young people for un-Islamic dress. The hardliners call them guardians of virtue. The magistrate asks whether to arrest the squads or quietly tolerate them.

left.label: Arrest the morality squads.
left.tooltip: The state polices the streets, not a self-righteous mob.
left.reveal: You jail the vigilantes and protect the harassed. The hardline preachers howl about defending vice, but the parks and streets become a little freer and a little safer.

right.label: Tolerate the morality patrols.
right.tooltip: Let the pious keep the streets decent.
right.reveal: You look away as the squads enforce their code. The hardliners are gratified, and a creeping, unofficial morality police takes root, answering to no law but its own.

speaker: Intelligence Chief
prompt: Investigators have located a secret detention site — an 'Aynaghar' of mirrored cells where the disappeared are held off the books. The story is about to break. The chief asks whether to deny everything and bury the site, or acknowledge it and free the captives.

left.label: Deny it and bury the site.
left.tooltip: A secret admitted is a secret weaponised.
left.reveal: You raze the site, scrub the records, and deny it ever existed. The denials collapse under the survivors' testimony, and the secret prison becomes the defining symbol of your rule.

right.label: Acknowledge it and free the captives.
right.tooltip: A crime confessed can at least be ended.
right.reveal: You shut the site, release the held, and promise an inquiry. The admission is a bombshell and the security establishment panics, but the disappeared come home and a monstrous chapter closes.

speaker: Hill Tracts Security Officer
prompt: In the hill tracts, the army's web of checkpoints and camps controls indigenous life and feeds a low-grade insurgency, while settlers and soldiers want the grip kept tight. The officer asks whether to draw down the military presence per the peace accord, or hold it.

left.label: Hold the heavy military grip.
left.tooltip: A drawdown invites the insurgents back.
left.reveal: You keep the camps and checkpoints in place. The settlers and brass are reassured, but indigenous resentment festers under the daily indignity, and the accord rots another year.

right.label: Draw down per the peace accord.
right.tooltip: A region held at gunpoint is never truly held.
right.reveal: You pull back camps and ease the checkpoints as the accord requires. The army and settlers warn of chaos, but the hill peoples breathe, and a buried peace process stirs.

speaker: District Magistrate
prompt: A doctored screenshot alleging a Hindu youth insulted the Prophet has set mobs torching a minority village. The youth may well be innocent and framed. The magistrate asks whether to protect the village and investigate the framing, or appease the mob by arresting the accused.

left.label: Protect the village, investigate the framing.
left.tooltip: A village burned on a forgery is a crime against us all.
left.reveal: You deploy force to shield the village and pursue whoever forged the post. The mob's instigators are exposed and jailed, and the framed youth is spared a fate sealed by a lie.

right.label: Arrest the accused to calm the mob.
right.tooltip: An arrest now is cheaper than a riot tonight.
right.reveal: You jail the accused youth to placate the crowd, guilt unproven. The village still smoulders, the real forgers go free, and the law bends, once again, to whoever can summon a mob.

speaker: Protocol Security Chief
prompt: A huge share of the police is tied up guarding ministers, MPs, and their families, stripping protection from ordinary neighbourhoods. The chief can slash the VIP details and redeploy to the public, or keep the entourages your elite has come to expect.

left.label: Slash VIP security, redeploy to the public.
left.tooltip: A constable guarding a minister's nephew is stolen from a slum.
left.reveal: You cut the bloated VIP details and push police back into the neighbourhoods. The elite fume at their shrunken entourages, but ordinary streets see a uniform again.

right.label: Keep the VIP protection as it is.
right.tooltip: An exposed minister is an unstable government.
right.reveal: You leave the entourages intact. The elite feel safe and flattered, and the ordinary neighbourhoods keep waiting for a police presence that is parked outside someone's mansion.

speaker: Cyber Patrol Commander
prompt: A new cyber-patrol unit can monitor social media around the clock and arrest citizens for 'anti-state' posts — memes, jokes, criticism. The commander wants a wide mandate; it would chill all online speech and fill the jails with teenagers over Facebook posts.

left.label: Give the cyber patrol a wide mandate.
left.tooltip: A nation's mood is made on its phones; watch them.
left.reveal: The unit trawls the feeds and the arrests for posts begin. Online criticism withers into silence, and the spectacle of teenagers jailed over memes draws global derision.

right.label: Restrict it to genuine threats only.
right.tooltip: A meme is not a crime, and a jail is not a comments section.
right.reveal: You confine the unit to real incitement and terror, not criticism. The dissent stays loud and irritating online, but the jails are not filled with children over Facebook posts.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: A court has ordered a vast slum cleared from contested land, and the police must execute it. The IGP can carry out a brutal dawn eviction with bulldozers, or slow-walk it and push for relocation and resettlement first.

left.label: Carry out the dawn eviction by force.
left.tooltip: A court order is a court order.
left.reveal: Bulldozers and baton charges clear the slum at first light. The land is cleared and the optics are savage — families weeping over rubble — and the resentment will outlast the headlines.

right.label: Slow-walk it, push for resettlement first.
right.tooltip: I will not bulldoze the poor into the street at dawn.
right.reveal: You delay enforcement and press for relocation sites before any clearance. The landowner and the court grow impatient, but the eviction, when it comes, does not begin with families in the rain.

speaker: Auxiliary Forces Director
prompt: The vast village auxiliary force could be armed and empowered as a rural security backbone — cheap manpower loyal to the state — or it risks becoming a politicised village militia settling local scores under colour of authority. The director awaits your call.

left.label: Arm and empower the village auxiliaries.
left.tooltip: A loyal force in every village is a state in every village.
left.reveal: You arm and expand the auxiliaries into a rural security web. Coverage spreads cheaply, and so does the abuse, as local commanders use their new authority to settle old scores.

right.label: Keep the auxiliaries unarmed and limited.
right.tooltip: An armed militia in every village is a thousand small tyrannies.
right.reveal: You keep the force unarmed and tightly bounded. Rural security leans more on the regular police, but you avoid seeding the countryside with armed men answering to local bosses.

speaker: Financial Crimes Chief
prompt: A sophisticated counterfeit-currency ring, run from across the border, is flooding the markets with fake notes and eroding trust in the cash economy. The chief can mount a costly crackdown and currency-upgrade, or let it run and quietly manage the damage.

left.label: Crack down and upgrade the currency.
left.tooltip: Fake money is a slow poison in the bloodstream.
left.reveal: You smash the distribution networks and roll out harder-to-forge notes. The operation is costly and disruptive at the tills, but trust in the cash in people's hands is restored.

right.label: Let it run and manage the damage.
right.tooltip: A currency overhaul is a budget I will not spend on a nuisance.
right.reveal: You leave the ring largely alone and absorb the losses. The fakes keep circulating, the cash economy frays at the edges, and the cross-border syndicate grows bolder.

speaker: Women's Safety Officer
prompt: Street harassment of girls and women — 'eve-teasing' that has driven some to suicide — is endemic and unpunished. The officer wants dedicated rapid-response units, mobile courts, and harassment made a real, enforced offence; the cultural pushback frames it as policing male nature.

left.label: Enforce harassment as a real crime.
left.tooltip: A girl afraid to walk to school is a society failing her.
left.reveal: You stand up rapid-response teams and mobile courts and start jailing harassers. The cultural grumbling is loud, but girls and women begin, cautiously, to reclaim the streets.

right.label: Leave it to social norms.
right.tooltip: The street cannot be policed for every catcall.
right.reveal: You decline to make it a priority. The harassment goes on unchecked, the streets stay hostile to half the population, and another schoolgirl's suicide note names names no one will pursue.

speaker: Counter-Terror Commander
prompt: Machete-wielding militants are hacking secular bloggers and publishers to death in the streets, and a hit-list circulates online. The commander wants the targets given protection and the cells hunted; hardliners hint the victims 'insulted faith' and deserve no sympathy.

left.label: Protect the targets and hunt the cells.
left.tooltip: A citizen's life is not forfeit for a sentence he wrote.
left.reveal: You assign protection to the listed and hunt the killers hard. The hardliners mutter that you defend blasphemers, but the murders slow and the state shows it shields even those it disagrees with.

right.label: Quietly let the matter lie.
right.tooltip: I will not spend the force defending men who provoke the pious.
right.reveal: You offer little protection and a soft pursuit. The hardliners are satisfied, the hit-list lengthens, and the world watches secular voices hacked down while the state looks away.

speaker: Police Reform Commissioner
prompt: Investigations here lean on confessions beaten out of suspects on remand, sending innocents to the gallows and letting real culprits walk. The commissioner wants forensic, evidence-based policing and an end to torture-remand; the old guard says it ties their hands.

left.label: End torture-remand, build forensic policing.
left.tooltip: A confession under torture convicts the innocent and frees the guilty.
left.reveal: You ban torture-remand and invest in forensics and training. The old guard complains their hands are tied, but wrongful convictions fall and real evidence starts to convict the real culprits.

right.label: Leave the remand culture intact.
right.tooltip: A force without remand is a force without leverage.
right.reveal: You leave the beat-a-confession culture untouched. Cases 'close' fast on coerced statements, innocents hang, and the real criminals — and the rot in the method — go on undisturbed.

speaker: Immigration Director
prompt: The passport and immigration offices run on bribes — fast-tracking the payers, stalling the honest, and quietly issuing genuine documents to criminals and traffickers. The director can digitise and clean the system or keep the lucrative counters humming.

left.label: Digitise and clean the passport system.
left.tooltip: A passport sold to a trafficker is a border breached.
left.reveal: You automate and audit the offices and the bribery withers. Honest applicants get served and the document-for-criminals trade is choked, and the counter staff who lived on bribes seethe.

right.label: Leave the passport counters as they are.
right.tooltip: A demoralised office processes nothing.
right.reveal: You leave the racket running. The bribes flow and the queues move for those who pay, and genuine passports keep finding their way into the hands of traffickers and fugitives.

speaker: Law Secretary
prompt: A condemned man's mercy petition sits on your desk amid real doubts about his trial — a coerced confession, a recanted witness. The base bays for the hanging; a quiet voice says executing a possibly innocent man is a stain that never washes out.

left.label: Let the execution proceed.
left.tooltip: The court has spoken; mercy is weakness.
left.reveal: You reject the petition and the man hangs. The base is satisfied, and the unexamined doubts about his guilt harden into a likely miscarriage you can never undo.

right.label: Commute the sentence pending review.
right.tooltip: A doubt this size cannot end on a gallows.
right.reveal: You commute the sentence and order the doubts examined. The base howls about coddling criminals, but you refuse to make the state the executioner of a man it may have wronged.

speaker: Fire Service Director
prompt: The capital's fire service cannot reach half its high-rises, its hydrants are dry, and its ladders are too short for the towers that keep burning with people trapped inside. The director needs a costly modernisation; the money is wanted elsewhere until the next blaze.

left.label: Fund the fire-service modernisation.
left.tooltip: A ladder too short is a coffin with a view.
left.reveal: You re-equip the fire service with tall ladders, working hydrants, and training. The cost is heavy and the danger feels abstract between fires, but the next tower blaze meets a service that can reach it.

right.label: Defer it until after the next disaster.
right.tooltip: The budget has louder claims than a quiet ladder.
right.reveal: You put the modernisation off. The money goes to noisier needs, and the next high-rise fire finds the same dry hydrants and short ladders, and the same trapped people at the windows.

speaker: Border Guard Director
prompt: Along the border, official 'haat' markets could legalise the petty cross-border trade that locals depend on and that smugglers now run. The director can expand the legal haats, undercutting the smuggling mafias, or keep the border sealed and the black trade thriving.

left.label: Expand the legal border markets.
left.tooltip: A legal stall starves an illegal smuggler.
left.reveal: You expand the border haats and the petty trade comes into the open. The smuggling mafias lose ground, border communities prosper, and tensions along the wire ease a little.

right.label: Keep the border sealed.
right.tooltip: An open market is an open door.
right.reveal: You keep the border tight and the haats few. Security looks robust on paper, and the smuggling mafias keep their monopoly on the trade the locals will pursue regardless.

speaker: Crime Investigation Chief
prompt: A wave of kidnappings-for-ransom is terrifying the business class, and some cases trace to off-duty police and political cadres. The chief can mount a real crackdown that reaches into your own ranks, or focus on the freelance gangs and spare the connected.

left.label: Crack down, your own ranks included.
left.tooltip: A kidnapper in uniform is the worst kind.
left.reveal: You pursue every thread, including the police and cadres involved. The business class is reassured and the connected are rattled and angry, but the kidnapping wave breaks.

right.label: Chase the freelancers, spare the connected.
right.tooltip: Some uniforms are better not investigated.
right.reveal: You round up the freelance gangs and leave the connected untouched. The headlines look tough, but the protected predators keep operating and the business class quietly moves its families abroad.

speaker: Rural Justice Officer
prompt: In the villages, informal arbitration councils — shalish — increasingly issue cruel, illegal punishments: lashings, hilla marriages, and worse, mostly against women, under the colour of religion. The officer wants them reined in by the formal courts; the rural clergy resist.

left.label: Rein in the shalish, enforce the law.
left.tooltip: A village court that lashes women is a crime, not a custom.
left.reveal: You move to subordinate the shalish to formal law and prosecute the cruellest verdicts. The rural clergy protest the intrusion, but women gain protection from punishments dressed as tradition.

right.label: Leave the village councils alone.
right.tooltip: I will not fight every village elder in the land.
right.reveal: You leave the shalish to their ways. The rural power-holders are placated, and the lashings, the hilla marriages, and the village 'justice' against women grind on beyond the law's reach.

speaker: Telecom Security Director
prompt: A new law would let your agencies intercept any call or message — with, or without, a judge's warrant. The director wants the warrantless version for speed; civil society demands judicial oversight to stop the inevitable abuse against critics and rivals.

left.label: Pass warrantless interception powers.
left.tooltip: A warrant is a delay a plot does not grant me.
left.reveal: You take the warrantless powers. The agencies move fast on real threats and faster on inconvenient critics, and the surveillance state gains a legal blank cheque that will outlast you.

right.label: Require judicial warrants for interception.
right.tooltip: A wiretap with no judge is a weapon with no safety.
right.reveal: You insist on warrants and oversight. The agencies grumble about delay, but interception keeps a check against the day it would otherwise be turned wholesale on dissent.

speaker: Highway Police Chief
prompt: Armed gangs are robbing night buses and trucks on the highways, sometimes killing, and the long-haul transport that moves the economy is gripped by fear. The chief wants costly highway patrols and checkpoints; others say it is a local nuisance not worth the budget.

left.label: Fund highway patrols and checkpoints.
left.tooltip: A highway ruled by dacoits is an economy held hostage.
left.reveal: You blanket the highways with patrols and the robberies fall. The cost is real and the checkpoints slow some traffic, but the night roads grow safe enough to move goods and people again.

right.label: Treat it as a minor local matter.
right.tooltip: A few highway robberies do not justify a national force.
right.reveal: You leave the highways lightly policed. The budget is spared, the dacoit gangs grow bolder, and the long-haul transport that feeds the cities runs scared and dear after dark.

speaker: Social Crime Officer
prompt: Organised syndicates maim children and traffic the disabled into forced begging rings on the city streets. The officer wants the rings smashed and the victims rehabilitated — a costly, complex rescue — or you can keep clearing beggars off the streets for the optics.

left.label: Smash the rings, rehabilitate the victims.
left.tooltip: A child maimed for alms is a crime crying out.
left.reveal: You break the begging syndicates and build rehabilitation for the rescued. The work is hard and costly and the rings fight back, but maimed children are freed instead of just swept from view.

right.label: Just clear beggars off the streets.
right.tooltip: The tourists and the elite want clean pavements.
right.reveal: You mount street-clearing drives for the optics. The pavements look tidier for visitors, the syndicates simply relocate, and the trafficked children keep being maimed for someone else's alms.

speaker: Protocol Security Chief
prompt: Every ministerial movement shuts down the capital's arteries for VIP motorcades, trapping ambulances and commuters for hours in the world's worst gridlock. The chief can curb the closures and the convoys, or keep the elite gliding through a paralysed city.

left.label: Curb the VIP road closures.
left.tooltip: A minister's ten minutes is not worth a city's hour.
left.reveal: You slash the motorcade privileges and the blanket closures. The elite chafe at sharing the roads, but ambulances move, commuters get home, and the city sees its rulers join the traffic.

right.label: Keep the VIP convoys gliding.
right.tooltip: A minister stuck in traffic is a state diminished.
right.reveal: You keep the motorcade closures. The elite glide through on cleared roads, and the rest of the capital — ambulances and all — sits choking in the gridlock the convoys leave behind.

speaker: Crime Investigation Chief
prompt: A shoddily-built tower has collapsed, killing scores, and the trail leads to a developer who bribed the building inspectors — a developer who funds the party. The chief asks whether to prosecute him fully or let the inspectors take the fall alone.

left.label: Prosecute the developer fully.
left.tooltip: A builder who buries his buyers must answer for it.
left.reveal: You charge the developer himself, donor or not. The party loses a funder and the building lobby trembles, but the bereaved see real accountability and the inspectors are not the only scapegoats.

right.label: Let the inspectors take the fall.
right.tooltip: A donor toppled topples a wing of the party.
right.reveal: You confine the blame to a few inspectors and shield the developer. The party keeps its funder, the building lobby relaxes, and the next shoddy tower is already rising on a bribed certificate.

speaker: Cyber Crime Chief
prompt: Scam operations are draining citizens' mobile wallets and bank accounts through phishing and fake-prize calls, some run from organised centres with insider help from telecom and bank staff. The chief wants a real cyber-fraud taskforce; standing it up is costly and technical.

left.label: Build a real cyber-fraud taskforce.
left.tooltip: A scam centre is a thief that never sleeps.
left.reveal: You fund a proper cyber-fraud unit with bank and telecom cooperation. The scam centres are raided and the insider leaks plugged, and citizens' savings grow a little safer online.

right.label: Leave it to overstretched local police.
right.tooltip: Online fraud is a nuisance, not a priority.
right.reveal: You leave cyber-fraud to thinly-spread local stations that cannot trace it. The scam centres flourish, the insider leaks continue, and the public learns that money lost online is money gone.

speaker: Public Order Officer
prompt: Competing religious processions are due to cross the same route on the same day, a flashpoint that has sparked riots before. The officer can impose strict separate routes and timings — heavy-handed and resented by both — or trust the organisers and keep policing light.

left.label: Impose strict separate routes.
left.tooltip: Two flashpoints kept apart make no fire.
left.reveal: You enforce separate routes and tight timings. Both communities grumble at the heavy hand, but the processions pass without collision and the day ends without a riot to mourn.

right.label: Trust the organisers, police lightly.
right.tooltip: A festival smothered in police is no festival.
right.reveal: You keep policing light and trust the community leaders. The day may pass in peace and harmony — or one hot-headed clash may ignite the riot the light touch failed to prevent.

speaker: Transport Security Coordinator
prompt: Before Eid, millions cram onto overloaded buses, trains, and ferries to reach their villages, and every year the holiday rush kills hundreds in crashes and capsizes. The coordinator wants strict overloading and safety enforcement; the public wants cheap passage home at any cost.

left.label: Enforce holiday travel safety hard.
left.tooltip: A homecoming should not end in a hearse.
left.reveal: You enforce capacity limits and roadworthiness through the rush. Fares rise and the crush at the terminals worsens, but the holiday's grim death toll on the roads and rivers falls sharply.

right.label: Let the holiday rush flow cheaply.
right.tooltip: The poor must reach home for Eid, rules or no.
right.reveal: You ease enforcement so everyone can afford to travel. The buses and ferries overload as always, the homecoming proceeds cheap and chaotic, and the holiday claims its usual harvest of the dead.

speaker: Crime Investigation Chief
prompt: Illegal firearms are awash in the country, fuelling political violence and crime. The chief proposes a no-questions surrender amnesty to mop them up — which also lets the guilty erase the weapons tying them to past crimes. The alternative is a hard, raid-based recovery drive.

left.label: Offer a no-questions arms amnesty.
left.tooltip: A gun surrendered is a gun that kills no one.
left.reveal: You run the amnesty and weapons pour in by the thousand. The streets grow a little safer, and a number of past crimes lose the evidence that might have solved them.

right.label: Mount a hard, raid-based recovery.
right.tooltip: An amnesty launders the gunman with his gun.
right.reveal: You hunt the illegal arms through raids and intelligence. Fewer weapons come in and the operation is dangerous and slow, but the guns recovered come with the cases attached to them.

speaker: Diplomatic Security Chief
prompt: After threats against foreigners, aid agencies and embassies demand heavy protection for their staff or they will pull out, taking their programmes and goodwill with them. The chief can lock the foreign community in a security bubble, or reassure with lighter, smarter measures.

left.label: Wrap foreigners in a heavy security bubble.
left.tooltip: A dead foreigner is a global headline I cannot afford.
left.reveal: You blanket the foreign community in visible heavy security. The agencies stay, but the fortress optics signal a dangerous country, and the bubble itself becomes a target and a symbol.

right.label: Reassure with lighter, smarter measures.
right.tooltip: A fortress says 'flee' louder than any threat.
right.reveal: You opt for intelligence-led, lighter protection and quiet reassurance. It is a calculated risk, but the agencies stay, the country looks governable rather than besieged, and normal life goes on.

speaker: Social Welfare Magistrate
prompt: Children in conflict with the law are thrown into adult jails and 'correction' centres rife with abuse, emerging hardened criminals. The magistrate wants a real juvenile-justice system of diversion and rehabilitation; the law-and-order voices want them treated as the threats they'll become.

left.label: Build a rehabilitative juvenile system.
left.tooltip: A child jailed with hardened men comes out a hardened man.
left.reveal: You build diversion, separate facilities, and rehabilitation for child offenders. The tough-on-crime crowd scoffs, but children get a path back instead of a finishing school for criminals.

right.label: Keep treating young offenders harshly.
right.tooltip: A young criminal is still a criminal.
right.reveal: You keep juveniles in the adult-adjacent system. It looks tough and costs little now, and each cohort of abused children graduates from the cells into the very crime the harshness was meant to stop.

speaker: Surveillance Technology Director
