prompt: A fleet of surveillance drones could watch borders and crime hotspots — and hover over every protest and gathering, mapping the crowd. The director wants the full fleet with crowd-monitoring; civil society warns it is an eye in the sky over every dissenter.

left.label: Deploy the full surveillance drone fleet.
left.tooltip: An eye in the sky misses nothing.
left.reveal: You acquire the drone fleet with crowd-monitoring. Borders and crime hotspots are watched, and so is every protest, every gathering, every face that ever assembles against you.

right.label: Limit drones to borders and disasters.
right.tooltip: A drone over every march is a state over every shoulder.
right.reveal: You confine the drones to borders, disasters, and crime scenes, barring protest surveillance. You gain real operational reach without building an aerial panopticon over public dissent.

speaker: Health Crime Officer
prompt: A ring is buying kidneys from desperate villagers — coerced and cheated — for wealthy transplant tourists, with crooked doctors and brokers in the chain. The officer wants the whole network smashed; some of the clinics involved are powerful and well-connected.

left.label: Smash the organ-trafficking network.
left.tooltip: A poor man's kidney is not a commodity.
left.reveal: You break the ring, clinics and brokers and all, however connected. The transplant-tourism money dries up and powerful doctors face the law, and the cheated villagers gain a defender.

right.label: Pursue only the village brokers.
right.tooltip: The clinics have patrons I cannot afford to cross.
right.reveal: You arrest the low-level brokers and spare the connected clinics. The visible end of the trade is disrupted, the lucrative clinical core survives, and the poor keep being harvested for parts.

speaker: Telecom Security Officer
prompt: Biometric SIM registration would tie every phone to a fingerprint and ID — curbing the anonymous numbers used in crime and terror, and building a perfect map of who holds every phone in the country. The officer wants it mandatory; privacy advocates are alarmed.

left.label: Mandate biometric SIM registration.
left.tooltip: An anonymous phone is a criminal's best friend.
left.reveal: You require fingerprint registration for every SIM. Anonymous crime-phones vanish, and the state acquires a fingerprint-linked map of every phone and, through it, every person who carries one.

right.label: Register SIMs without the biometrics.
right.tooltip: A fingerprint for a phone number is a price too high.
right.reveal: You require ID but bar the biometric linkage. You gain most of the accountability without building a national fingerprint-to-phone database ripe for abuse.

speaker: Internal Affairs Director
prompt: Deaths in police custody, always ruled 'heart attacks' or 'suicides', are mounting and the families are organising. The director can establish a genuinely independent inquiry mechanism, exposing the torture behind the deaths, or keep the in-house whitewash that protects the force.

left.label: Establish independent custodial-death inquiries.
left.tooltip: A death in a cell the state cannot explain is a death the state caused.
left.reveal: You set up independent inquiries with real powers. The force resists and a few careers end, but the era of automatic 'heart attack' verdicts cracks, and custody grows a little less lethal.

right.label: Keep the in-house inquiries.
right.tooltip: An independent inquiry indicts the whole force.
right.reveal: You keep custodial deaths investigated by the police themselves. The verdicts stay tidy and the force protected, and the families learn that a death in custody here has only one official cause: misfortune.

speaker: River Police Chief
prompt: Armed pirates rule stretches of the great rivers and the bay, robbing and ransoming fishermen and cargo boats, sometimes with bribed police looking on. The chief wants a real river-police push; it means costly boats and confronting the officers on the take.

left.label: Fund a real anti-piracy river force.
left.tooltip: A river ruled by pirates is a river lost.
left.reveal: You fund fast boats and clean out the complicit officers. The pirate gangs are broken, the fishermen and cargo boats breathe, and the river patrols' own corruption is purged.

right.label: Leave the rivers lightly patrolled.
right.tooltip: A few river pirates do not justify a navy.
right.reveal: You leave the rivers thinly policed. The budget is saved, the pirate gangs and their police protectors thrive, and the fishermen pay their ransoms and their bribes as a cost of the water.

speaker: Immigration Director
prompt: You can quietly add critics, activists, and inconvenient businessmen to an airport exit-control list, trapping them in the country without charge or notice. The director calls it a clean, deniable leash; civil society calls it abduction by paperwork.

left.label: Bar the critics from leaving.
left.tooltip: A man who cannot flee can be reached.
left.reveal: You list the critics and they are turned back at the airport, bewildered. The leash is effective and deniable, until the pattern leaks and 'jailed within their own country' becomes the headline.

right.label: Keep the exit list to genuine fugitives.
right.tooltip: An exit ban on a critic is a confession of fear.
right.reveal: You confine the list to actual suspects with cases. The critics keep their freedom to come and go, and you are spared the spectacle of dissidents stranded at the departure gate.

speaker: Education Board Chairman
prompt: National exam papers leak online before every sitting, sold by a ring reaching into the printing presses and the boards. The chairman wants a hard crackdown and a digital question system; the leak ring is lucrative and protected, and a botched reform could throw the whole exam into chaos.

left.label: Crack down and digitise the exams.
left.tooltip: An exam sold in advance is a generation cheated.
left.reveal: You break the leak ring and move to secure digital question delivery. The protected racket fights back and one rollout stumbles, but the exams stop being an open auction and merit means something again.

right.label: Patch the system and hope.
right.tooltip: A radical overhaul could collapse the whole exam.
right.reveal: You make cosmetic fixes and leave the ring largely intact. The exams proceed, the papers keep leaking to those who pay, and the honest student keeps losing to the connected cheat.

speaker: Public Service Commission Liaison
prompt: Government job exams — the holy grail for millions of youth — are riddled with leaked papers, impersonators, and posts sold to the highest bidder. The liaison can mount a real anti-fraud overhaul, exposing connected beneficiaries, or keep the lucrative status quo.

left.label: Overhaul the recruitment exams.
left.tooltip: A job bought is a youth's dream stolen.
left.reveal: You secure the exams with biometrics and audits and void the fraudulent results. The connected who bought their posts are exposed and furious, but a generation sees that merit might finally count.

right.label: Leave the recruitment racket running.
right.tooltip: Too many of the bought belong to the party.
right.reveal: You leave the exams compromised. The posts keep going to the connected and the payers, and the honest, brilliant youth who studied for years learn what their merit is worth here.

speaker: Narcotics Control Officer
prompt: Bootleg toxic liquor has killed dozens of the poor in a single district, and the trade — banned, so unregulated — is run by syndicates with police protection. The officer can crack down hard on the bootleggers, or push the harder path of regulating and taxing safe alcohol.

left.label: Crack down hard on the bootleggers.
left.tooltip: Poison sold as drink is murder by the bottle.
left.reveal: You raid the bootleg syndicates and their police protectors. The killer batches are seized, but prohibition stays, the trade regroups underground, and the next toxic batch is already brewing.

right.label: Regulate and tax safe alcohol.
right.tooltip: Banning a drink only hands it to poisoners.
right.reveal: You move toward regulating and taxing safe alcohol, draining the bootleggers' market. The pious are scandalised and the politics are hard, but the poor stop dying from methanol in a bottle.

speaker: Prisons Inspector-General
prompt: The women's wings of the jails are overcrowded and abusive, holding women on petty or fabricated charges, some with their children, in degrading conditions. The IG wants reform — separate facilities, legal aid, release of the wrongly held — at real cost and effort.

left.label: Reform the women's prisons.
left.tooltip: A jail that degrades women degrades the state that runs it.
left.reveal: You fund better facilities, legal aid, and a review that frees the wrongly held. The cost and effort are real, but women and their children stop rotting in conditions no law intended.

right.label: Leave the women's wings as they are.
right.tooltip: The prisons have louder problems than comfort.
right.reveal: You leave the women's wings to their squalor. The budget is spared, and women on petty and fabricated charges keep languishing, their children growing up behind bars beside them.

speaker: Cyber Crime Chief
prompt: Foreign-run online betting and casino apps are hooking the young and draining money abroad through illegal channels, with local agents and some officials taking a cut. The chief can block and prosecute hard, or quietly tolerate a trade that greases many palms.

left.label: Block and prosecute the gambling networks.
left.tooltip: An app that bankrupts the young is a crime, not a game.
left.reveal: You block the apps, choke the payment channels, and prosecute the local agents. The trade is disrupted and the capital flight slowed, and the officials who took their cut go quiet and resentful.

right.label: Tolerate the gambling trade.
right.tooltip: A vice this profitable has many friends.
right.reveal: You leave the betting apps largely alone. The palms stay greased and the trade flourishes, and the young keep gambling their families' money into accounts somewhere overseas.

speaker: Forensic Services Director
prompt: The country's one overwhelmed forensic lab has a years-long backlog, so DNA and ballistic evidence arrives long after trials end, and justice runs on confessions instead of science. The director needs heavy investment in labs and trained scientists; the payoff is slow and invisible.

left.label: Invest heavily in forensic capacity.
left.tooltip: Justice without science is justice by guesswork.
left.reveal: You fund new labs, equipment, and forensic scientists. The backlog shrinks over years, evidence starts reaching trials in time, and the reliance on beaten confessions slowly gives way to proof.

right.label: Leave the forensic system starved.
right.tooltip: Labs are a luxury; confessions are free.
right.reveal: You leave the single lab to drown in its backlog. The budget is spared, and the courts keep convicting on confession and guesswork while the science gathers dust in an evidence locker.

speaker: Police Procurement Chief
prompt: A foreign vendor offers a large package of riot-control gear — water cannon, rubber bullets, tear gas, sound cannons. The chief wants the full arsenal for crowd control; critics note that 'less-lethal' tools, in untrained hands, still blind and kill protesters.

left.label: Buy the full riot-control arsenal.
left.tooltip: A well-equipped force controls a crowd without a massacre.
left.reveal: You acquire the arsenal. Crowd control gets a notch less lethal in theory, and in untrained hands the rubber bullets and gas still maim and kill, just with deniability.

right.label: Buy modest gear with strict training.
right.tooltip: A weapon is only as humane as the hand that wields it.
right.reveal: You buy a modest kit and invest in restraint and training. The arsenal is smaller, but the officers learn to use it as a last resort, and crowd policing grows genuinely less deadly.

speaker: Women's Crime Officer
prompt: A young bride has been killed over an unpaid dowry, and the in-laws — locally powerful — are buying off the police and the witnesses as such cases routinely vanish. The officer asks whether to drive the prosecution hard or let it fade like the others.

left.label: Drive the dowry-murder prosecution.
left.tooltip: A bride burned for money will have her day in court.
left.reveal: You protect the witnesses and push the case against the powerful in-laws. They lean on every connection and lose, and for once a dowry murder ends in a verdict instead of a settlement.

right.label: Let the case fade like the others.
right.tooltip: The in-laws have friends I would rather keep.
right.reveal: You let the case dissolve in bought witnesses and lost files. The powerful in-laws walk, the dead bride joins the uncounted, and every dowry-taker learns the price of a daughter-in-law's life.

speaker: Telecom Security Director
prompt: Citizens are using VPNs to slip past your website blocks and reach banned news and platforms. The director wants VPNs themselves blocked and their users tracked, sealing the information bubble; the alternative is to accept that the net cannot be fully walled.

left.label: Block VPNs and track their users.
left.tooltip: A wall with a tunnel under it is no wall.
left.reveal: You move to block VPNs and flag their users. The information bubble tightens toward North-Korean completeness, the tech-savvy find new tunnels anyway, and the censorship draws global scorn.

right.label: Accept that the net cannot be sealed.
right.tooltip: A government at war with VPNs is a government afraid of news.
right.reveal: You stop chasing VPNs. The blocks stay porous and the banned sites reachable, but you are spared the futile, reputation-shredding crusade against the open internet.

speaker: Social Welfare Director
prompt: Tens of thousands of children live on the city streets — sniffing glue, prey to gangs, traffickers, and abusive police. The director wants a real network of shelters, schooling, and family reunification; the cheaper option is the periodic round-up that just moves them on.

left.label: Build shelters and reunification programmes.
left.tooltip: A child on the street is a future thrown away.
left.reveal: You build shelters, schooling, and family-tracing for street children. The cost is real and the work slow, but children are pulled off the streets into something like a childhood.

right.label: Stick with periodic round-ups.
right.tooltip: A shelter network is a budget I do not have.
right.reveal: You keep the cheap round-up drives that scoop children up and dump them elsewhere. The streets look briefly clearer, and the children drift back to the glue, the gangs, and the traffickers.

speaker: Cyber Crime Chief
prompt: Intimate images — real and faked — are being weaponised to blackmail and shame women and girls, driving some to suicide, while victims who report are themselves blamed and exposed. The chief wants a dedicated, confidential unit; building it means resources and a cultural fight.

left.label: Build a confidential image-abuse unit.
left.tooltip: A girl shamed to death by a screen deserved a state that fought for her.
left.reveal: You stand up a confidential unit that pursues the blackmailers and protects the victims. The cultural pushback is real, but women begin to report without being destroyed for it.

right.label: Leave it to ordinary stations.
right.tooltip: These are private matters, not police priorities.
right.reveal: You leave image-abuse to the ordinary stations that blame and expose the victims. The blackmailers operate freely, and the suicides keep coming, each one a girl the state would not protect.

speaker: Public Order Officer
prompt: A massive open-air concert and several large public events are planned for the same weekend, with crowd-safety planning that is, as ever, an afterthought. The officer wants proper crowd management and caps imposed; the organisers and the public resent the fences and limits.

left.label: Impose proper crowd-safety management.
left.tooltip: A joyous crowd can become a crush in a heartbeat.
left.reveal: You enforce capacity caps, exits, and crowd management. The organisers grumble at the cost and the public at the fences, but the weekend's celebrations end without a stampede to bury.

right.label: Let the events run loosely.
right.tooltip: Do not smother a celebration in barriers.
right.reveal: You keep the events open and lightly managed. The crowds flow free and vast and joyous, and the absence of a plan means one surge from turning a festival into a funeral.

speaker: Rural Justice Officer
prompt: In remote areas, people — usually poor women — are branded witches or sorcerers and beaten, exiled, or killed by mobs, often at a 'healer's' word. The officer wants awareness drives and hard prosecution; the local power structure shrugs it off as backward custom.

left.label: Prosecute and educate against the violence.
left.tooltip: A woman killed as a witch is a murder, not a custom.
left.reveal: You prosecute the mob leaders and run awareness drives with the rural clergy and schools. Change is slow against deep superstition, but the killings draw consequences and the accused gain protectors.

right.label: Dismiss it as backward custom.
right.tooltip: I cannot police every village's superstitions.
right.reveal: You leave the matter to the locals. The 'healers' keep naming witches, the mobs keep their impunity, and poor women keep being beaten and burned for crimes that do not exist.

speaker: NID Database Director
prompt: Genuine national ID cards are being sold to non-citizens — including, the spies warn, to Rohingya and foreign criminals — through corrupt officials, eroding the integrity of the whole citizenship system. The director can purge and prosecute, or quietly manage the embarrassment.

left.label: Purge the fraud and prosecute officials.
left.tooltip: A citizenship sold is a nation cheapened.
left.reveal: You audit the database, cancel the fraudulent IDs, and jail the officials who sold them. The system's integrity is restored at the cost of a noisy scandal and some genuinely caught-up innocents.

right.label: Quietly manage the embarrassment.
right.tooltip: A public purge advertises how porous we are.
right.reveal: You handle it discreetly, cancelling a few cards and hushing the rest. The scandal stays small, and the corrupt officials and the trade in genuine IDs to non-citizens carry quietly on.

speaker: Registrar General
prompt: Marriage registrars routinely falsify brides' ages to legalise child marriages and forge documents for a fee, and the registry is a den of petty corruption. The registrar general wants digitised, age-verified registration; the registrars, a connected guild, will resist.

left.label: Digitise and age-verify marriage registration.
left.tooltip: A registrar who forges a child's age is an accomplice to her ruin.
left.reveal: You digitise registration with hard age verification. The registrars' guild howls at the lost fees, but child marriages lose their paper cover and the registry stops abetting them.

right.label: Leave the registrars to their trade.
right.tooltip: A connected guild is not lightly disturbed.
right.reveal: You leave the marriage registry as it is. The registrars keep their fees and their forgeries, and the falsified ages keep legalising the marriage of children across the country.

speaker: Narcotics Control Chief
prompt: Hundreds of thousands are addicted, and the few rehab centres are squalid, abusive, and out of reach of the poor. The chief wants a real, humane treatment network as the backbone of drug policy; the law-and-order voices still want jails and crossfire, not clinics.

left.label: Build a humane treatment network.
left.tooltip: An addict is a patient, not a body for the morgue.
left.reveal: You build accessible, humane rehabilitation as the spine of drug policy. The tough-on-drugs crowd scoffs, but families get their children back from addiction instead of from the river.

right.label: Stick with jails and crackdowns.
right.tooltip: Treatment coddles the weak; fear deters the rest.
right.reveal: You keep drug policy punitive. The squalid centres stay squalid and the addicted cycle through cells and crossfire, and the addiction the crackdowns were meant to crush keeps spreading.

speaker: District Police Superintendent
prompt: Armed 'sand mafias' — illegally dredging riverbeds and extorting the trade — rule whole stretches of riverbank with bribed police and political cover, killing officials who interfere. The superintendent asks whether to break them or keep the uneasy, lucrative peace.

left.label: Break the sand mafias.
left.tooltip: A river ruled by armed thieves is a writ of failure.
left.reveal: You move hard against the sand mafias and the officers and politicians shielding them. It is dangerous — they fight and kill — but the riverbanks are wrested back and the racket's cover is torn off.

right.label: Keep the uneasy peace.
right.tooltip: The men who run the sand also run the wards.
right.reveal: You leave the sand mafias to their riverbanks. The peace holds and the cover stays, and the illegal dredging guts the rivers while anyone who objects risks a body in the current.

speaker: Anti-Encroachment Officer
prompt: A drive to clear illegal encroachments from canals, railways, and public land keeps bulldozing the slums of the poor while the grand illegal buildings of the powerful stand untouched. The officer asks whether to turn the drive on the big fish or keep it pointed at the small.

left.label: Turn the eviction drive on the powerful.
left.tooltip: A bulldozer that only finds the poor is a class weapon.
left.reveal: You redirect the drive at the powerful's illegal grabs — the riverside mansions, the encroaching towers. The connected scream and litigate, but the law's bulldozer finally faces upward.

right.label: Keep clearing the poor's encroachments.
right.tooltip: The big buildings have lawyers; the slums do not.
right.reveal: You keep the drive pointed at the slums and hawkers. The optics of progress are cheap and easy, the powerful's grand encroachments stand serene, and the poor learn whose law this is.

speaker: Public Order Officer
prompt: A brutal summer of power cuts has people pouring into the streets, smashing substations and blocking roads in the heat. The officer wants a firm dispersal of the protests; the wiser path may be to be seen sharing the pain and fixing the supply.

left.label: Disperse the power-cut protests firmly.
left.tooltip: A mob at the substation is a mob, whatever its grievance.
left.reveal: You break up the protests with force. The streets clear and the substations are guarded, but beating people over power cuts they did not cause only stokes the anger in the dark.

right.label: Share the pain, prioritise the fix.
right.tooltip: You cannot baton a country into cool patience.
right.reveal: You pull police back, publicise load-shedding equally for the elite too, and rush repairs. The protests cool as people see the burden shared, and the anger drains toward patience.

speaker: Aviation Security Chief
prompt: The main airport leaks gold, drugs, and contraband through a web of complicit ground staff, customs, and security — and a lax screening regime that also worries foreign airlines about terror risk. The chief can mount a deep clean-up or keep the comfortable rot.

left.label: Deep-clean airport security.
left.tooltip: An airport for sale is a border for sale.
left.reveal: You overhaul screening and purge the complicit staff. Foreign airlines relax, the smuggling chokes, and the ground-staff syndicates and their patrons lose a goldmine and turn on you.

right.label: Leave the airport rackets running.
right.tooltip: A purge would ground half the staff and the flights.
right.reveal: You leave the airport's shadow economy intact. The flights run smoothly and the rackets thrive, and the gold and drugs keep flowing while foreign carriers quietly downgrade their security rating of you.

speaker: Prisons Inspector-General
prompt: The jails, packed to bursting, could ease overcrowding and idleness through a humane work-and-skills programme that prepares inmates for release — or descend into the exploitative forced labour that some officers already run for private profit.

left.label: Build a humane prison work-and-skills scheme.
left.tooltip: A prisoner taught a trade returns a citizen.
left.reveal: You build a real rehabilitation-through-work programme with wages and training. The reform is costly and the corrupt officers lose their private labour racket, but inmates leave with a skill instead of a grudge.

right.label: Let the labour rackets run.
right.tooltip: A busy prisoner is a quiet prisoner.
right.reveal: You leave prison labour to the officers' private schemes. Some inmates are quietly exploited for profit, the overcrowding and idleness fester, and the jails keep manufacturing hardened, embittered men.

speaker: Returnee Affairs Officer
prompt: A Gulf downturn has sent hundreds of thousands of jobless migrant workers home, broke and traumatised, into communities with no work for them — a security and humanitarian time bomb. The officer wants a reintegration and credit programme; the cheaper path is to let them fend for themselves.

left.label: Fund returnee reintegration and credit.
left.tooltip: A returnee abandoned is a grievance recruited.
left.reveal: You build reintegration support — credit, retraining, counselling — for the returnees. The cost is real, but a destabilising mass of broke, angry men is turned toward livelihoods instead of unrest.

right.label: Leave returnees to fend for themselves.
right.tooltip: The state did not send them; it cannot catch them all.
right.reveal: You leave the returnees to their own devices. The budget is spared, and a vast cohort of jobless, indebted, traumatised men sinks into despair, crime, and the recruiters' waiting arms.

speaker: Diplomatic Security Chief
prompt: Intelligence warns of a credible plot against a foreign embassy in the diplomatic enclave. The chief can flood the zone with a heavy, visible cordon that reassures the foreigners but screams danger, or run a quiet, intelligence-led protection that risks being caught short.

left.label: Flood the enclave with a visible cordon.
left.tooltip: A guarded embassy is a standing embassy.
left.reveal: You ring the enclave with heavy security. The diplomats are reassured and the plot likely deterred, but the fortress optics broadcast a country under siege and unsettle the very investors you court.

right.label: Run quiet, intelligence-led protection.
right.tooltip: A wall of guns advertises the fear it answers.
right.reveal: You opt for discreet, intelligence-driven security and roll up the plotters quietly. It is a gamble on your spies being right, but the enclave stays calm and the country looks composed.

speaker: Aviation Security Chief
prompt: A new aviation watchlist could bar suspected militants from flying — a real security tool, and, applied loosely, a way to ground journalists and activists under a terror label. The chief asks whether to build it tight and judicial, or broad and discretionary.

left.label: Build a broad, discretionary watchlist.
left.tooltip: Better a hundred grounded than one bomber boarding.
left.reveal: You build a broad watchlist with loose criteria. A few genuine threats are grounded, and so are journalists and activists who find themselves quietly barred from boarding under a label they cannot contest.

right.label: Build a tight, judicially-reviewed list.
right.tooltip: A watchlist with no judge is a blacklist with wings.
right.reveal: You build a narrow list with judicial review and appeal. It is slower and the security hawks complain, but it stops the terror label from becoming a travel ban on dissent.

speaker: District Police Superintendent
prompt: A proposal would require every cybercafe and gaming den to log users' IDs and install monitoring software, ostensibly against online crime and radicalisation. The superintendent likes the reach; critics see another net cast over ordinary young people.

left.label: Mandate ID-logging and monitoring.
left.tooltip: A screen unwatched is a plot unseen.
left.reveal: You require the logs and the software. A few real cases surface, and every student who plays a game or reads the news in a cybercafe now does so under the state's recorded eye.

right.label: Leave the cybercafes unmonitored.
right.tooltip: A boy at a screen is not a suspect.
right.reveal: You drop the surveillance mandate. You forgo a thin investigative edge, but the cybercafes stay places of private curiosity rather than logged interrogation rooms.

speaker: Women's Crime Officer
prompt: An acid attack has disfigured a young woman who spurned a powerful man's son, and the family is pressuring the police to lose the case as they have lost others. The officer asks whether to drive a hard prosecution and survivor support, or let it quietly die.

left.label: Drive the prosecution and fund survivor care.
left.tooltip: A face melted for a refusal will have its reckoning.
left.reveal: You protect the witnesses, fund the survivor's reconstruction, and push the case against the powerful family. They lean and lose, and an acid attacker faces the gallows the law prescribes.

right.label: Let the case quietly die.
right.tooltip: The boy's father is a friend I cannot lose.
right.reveal: You let the file dissolve. The disfigured woman gets neither justice nor care, the powerful family walks, and every spurned man with acid and connections takes note.

speaker: Crime Investigation Chief
prompt: Families of the missing — some lost to crime, some to your own agencies — are turned away from police stations that refuse even to file a report. The chief wants a mandatory missing-persons registry and real investigation; it would also document the disappearances the state would rather forget.

left.label: Build a real missing-persons system.
left.tooltip: A family that cannot even file a report has been failed twice.
left.reveal: You mandate prompt reporting, a public registry, and real investigation. Genuine missing-persons cases get traction, and so does an uncomfortable record of those who vanished into the state's own hands.

right.label: Leave the stations to turn families away.
right.tooltip: A registry of the missing is a registry of our sins.
right.reveal: You leave the practice untouched. The families keep being turned from the gates, the missing stay officially un-missing, and the disappearances leave no paper trail to haunt you.

speaker: Police Welfare Director
prompt: The rank-and-file police are overworked, underpaid, and crammed into squalid barracks, which feeds both their brutality and their corruption. The director wants real investment in pay, housing, and hours; the money is large and the public sees only the brutality it would soften.

left.label: Invest in police welfare and pay.
left.tooltip: A constable treated like dirt treats the public like dirt.
left.reveal: You fund better pay, housing, and humane hours. The cost is heavy and the public initially grudging, but morale lifts, and a force that is not desperate leans a little less on the baton and the bribe.

right.label: Leave the rank-and-file to grind.
right.tooltip: The budget has worthier claims than a policeman's pillow.
right.reveal: You leave the constabulary in its squalor. The money is saved, and the overworked, underpaid force keeps taking its frustration out on the public and its shortfall out of their pockets.

speaker: Intelligence Chief
prompt: Pressure mounts — at home and from abroad — to account for the men your agencies disappeared over the years. The chief can quietly begin returning those still alive and acknowledging the dead, or keep denying that any 'enforced disappearances' ever occurred.

left.label: Keep denying everything.
left.tooltip: An admission is a noose I tie for myself.
left.reveal: You maintain the blanket denial. The denials grow more threadbare as survivors and bodies surface, and the disappearances calcify into the defining crime of your rule.

right.label: Begin returning and accounting for them.
right.tooltip: A wound this deep can only close with the truth.
right.reveal: You quietly release the living and acknowledge the dead, families weeping at gates and gravesides. The security establishment panics at the precedent, but a monstrous chapter begins, at last, to close.

speaker: Hill Tracts Security Officer
prompt: At the hill-district checkpoints, indigenous travellers are routinely searched, shaken down, and humiliated, while settlers pass freely — a daily grind of indignity feeding the old insurgency. The officer asks whether to reform the checkpoints' conduct or keep the hard hand.

left.label: Reform checkpoint conduct.
left.tooltip: A people frisked daily learns to hate daily.
left.reveal: You enforce conduct rules, end the shakedowns, and treat hill travellers as citizens. The hard-liners in the brass grumble, but the daily humiliation eases and a grievance loses some of its heat.

right.label: Keep the hard hand at the checkpoints.
right.tooltip: A loosened grip in the hills invites the gun.
right.reveal: You leave the checkpoints to their ways. The settlers pass and the indigenous are shaken down, and every humiliation at the barrier quietly recruits for the insurgency the hard hand was meant to deter.

speaker: Drug Administration Chief
prompt: Counterfeit and substandard medicines — chalk antibiotics, fake cancer drugs — are killing patients and flooding the pharmacies through a ring with reach into licensed distributors. The chief wants a hard crackdown and track-and-trace; the trade is lucrative and connected.

left.label: Crack down and mandate track-and-trace.
left.tooltip: A fake pill is a slow bullet.
left.reveal: You smash the counterfeit ring and require track-and-trace on medicines. The connected distributors fight back, but the fake drugs recede from the pharmacy shelves and patients stop dying from chalk.

right.label: Leave the trade lightly policed.
right.tooltip: A crackdown would disrupt the whole supply.
right.reveal: You leave the counterfeit trade largely alone. The pharmacies stay stocked and the racket profitable, and patients keep swallowing chalk antibiotics and fake cancer drugs and quietly dying of treatable things.

speaker: Police Reform Commissioner
prompt: Plainclothes officers in unmarked vehicles routinely 'pick up' suspects without warrants, identification, or any record, leaving families frantic and the line between arrest and abduction erased. The commissioner wants mandatory identification and logging; the agencies call it a fatal delay.

left.label: Require ID, warrants, and logging for arrests.
left.tooltip: An arrest with no record is an abduction with a badge.
left.reveal: You mandate identification, paperwork, and immediate logging for every arrest. The agencies complain about lost speed and surprise, but the line between policing and disappearance is redrawn.

right.label: Leave the plainclothes pick-ups alone.
right.tooltip: An officer slowed by paperwork is an officer outrun.
right.reveal: You leave the unmarked-car arrests unregulated. The agencies keep their speed and their shadows, and the families keep standing at police gates that deny the person was ever taken.

speaker: District Police Superintendent
prompt: Before every festival, your party's local cadres run a 'collection' — extorting shopkeepers and transporters under the guise of donations, with the police looking on. The superintendent asks whether to stop the racket or let the loyal foot-soldiers feed.

left.label: Stop the festival extortion racket.
left.tooltip: A shopkeeper shaken down in my name curses my name.
left.reveal: You order the police to stop the collections and arrest the worst extortionists. The cadres feel betrayed and the local bosses grumble, but the traders breathe and the party's name stops being a threat.

right.label: Let the cadres make their collection.
right.tooltip: The foot-soldiers must eat between elections.
right.reveal: You leave the racket alone. The cadres feed and stay loyal, and every festival the shopkeepers and truckers pay a tax to the party's name while the police watch and wet their beaks.

speaker: Law Secretary
prompt: A revised anti-terrorism law could define 'terrorism' so broadly that protest, strikes, and dissent fall within it — a flexible weapon — or narrowly, confined to actual violence. The secretary asks how wide to draw the net.

left.label: Draw the terror definition broadly.
left.tooltip: A wide net catches the threat before it forms.
left.reveal: You pass a sweeping definition that sweeps in dissent. Genuine plots and peaceful critics alike now risk the terror label, and the law becomes your most flexible cudgel and a global byword for overreach.

right.label: Confine terrorism to actual violence.
right.tooltip: A law that calls a strike terror is a law against the people.
right.reveal: You keep the definition tight to real violence. The security hawks complain it ties their hands, but protest and dissent stay outside the terror net, and the law keeps its legitimacy.

speaker: Fire Investigation Officer
prompt: A massive fire has gutted a slum on land a developer covets, and the residents swear it was arson to drive them out — a pattern seen before. The officer can mount a real arson investigation reaching toward the developer, or rule it an accidental electrical fire and move on.

left.label: Investigate the fire as possible arson.
left.tooltip: A slum that burns whenever a developer wants it is no accident.
left.reveal: You launch a real investigation pointing at the land's would-be owner. The developer and his patrons push hard, but the residents see the state take their suspicion seriously for once.

right.label: Rule it an accidental electrical fire.
right.tooltip: An arson case against a developer is a war I need not start.
right.reveal: You close it as a wiring accident. The developer gets his cleared land in due course, the displaced scatter, and the convenient slum fire joins a long line of convenient slum fires.

speaker: Public Order Officer
prompt: The Hindu, Buddhist, and Christian communities' festivals approach amid online threats and a recent temple attack. The officer can deploy visible, generous protection for the minority celebrations, or keep it minimal to avoid 'provoking' the hardliners.

left.label: Protect the minority festivals generously.
left.tooltip: A festival that needs guarding will be guarded.
left.reveal: You deploy real protection around the temples, monasteries, and churches. The hardliners mutter about appeasement, but the minorities celebrate in peace and see a state that counts them as its own.

right.label: Keep minority protection minimal.
right.tooltip: I will not garrison every shrine and provoke the majority.
right.reveal: You provide token security. The hardliners are unprovoked and content, and the minorities celebrate under a shadow, one rumour away from the attack the thin protection cannot stop.

speaker: Camp Security Chief
prompt: To control the sprawling refugee camps, you can ring them with barbed wire and watchtowers, sealing the refugees in — easing local security fears, and drawing the agencies' charge that you have built an open-air prison for a stateless people.

left.label: Fence and seal the camps.
left.tooltip: An unfenced camp leaks crime and militancy into my towns.
left.reveal: You wire in the camps. The host communities' fears ease and movement is controlled, and the UN and rights world condemn the caging of a stateless people who fled exactly this.

right.label: Manage the camps without sealing them.
right.tooltip: A people who fled a cage should not find another.
right.reveal: You manage security with policing and registration rather than barbed wire. The host communities fret about the open edges, but you avoid building the prison-camp optics the agencies dread.

speaker: Police Reform Commissioner
prompt: The local police stations are intimidating, corrupt, and hostile, especially to the poor and to women, who would sooner suffer than enter one. The commissioner proposes citizen-friendly stations — women's help desks, complaint tracking, clean reception — at real cost and against deep cultural inertia.

left.label: Modernise the stations as citizen services.
left.tooltip: A thana the poor fear protects no one.
left.reveal: You rebuild the stations around service — women's desks, tracked complaints, accountable reception. The old culture resists, but ordinary people start to see the police as something other than a threat.

right.label: Leave the thana culture as it is.
right.tooltip: A police station is meant to be feared.
right.reveal: You leave the stations intimidating and opaque. The money is saved, and the poor and women keep avoiding the very institution meant to protect them, suffering crimes rather than reporting them.

speaker: Intelligence Chief
prompt: Intelligence wants to plant informants in mosques and religious gatherings to catch radicalisation early. It is a real counter-terror tool, and a profound intrusion that, exposed, would enrage the devout and confirm every fear of a surveillance state aimed at the faith.

left.label: Plant informants in religious gatherings.
left.tooltip: Radicalisation is preached before it is plotted.
left.reveal: You seed the mosques with informants. A few cells are caught early, and when the network is exposed — as it will be — the devout erupt at a state that spies on prayer itself.

right.label: Rely on community engagement instead.
right.tooltip: A spy in the mosque makes an enemy of the congregation.
right.reveal: You work through trusted community engagement rather than informants. The intelligence is thinner, but you do not pit the entire devout population against a state caught surveilling their worship.

speaker: Home Affairs Coordinator
prompt: A communal riot has left a minority neighbourhood gutted and several dead, with credible claims that police stood by or joined in. The coordinator asks whether to mount an independent inquiry and full compensation, or a quiet internal review that shields the force.

left.label: Hold an independent inquiry, compensate fully.
left.tooltip: A pogrom the police watched is a pogrom the state owns.
left.reveal: You order an independent inquiry and full reparations. The force is rattled and a few officers fall, but the minority community sees accountability and the message lands that the police are not above the law.

right.label: Hold a quiet internal review.
right.tooltip: An independent inquiry indicts the whole force.
right.reveal: You keep the review in-house and it absolves the police. The compensation is token, the officers protected, and the minority neighbourhood learns that those who burned it and those who watched will answer to no one.

speaker: Cyber Crime Chief
prompt: After the quota unrest organised online, the cyber unit wants to monitor student social-media networks and flag 'instigators' for pre-emptive action. The chief calls it early warning; it is also a chilling watch over a whole generation's political speech.

left.label: Monitor and pre-empt student networks.
left.tooltip: A movement watched in the cradle is a movement controlled.
left.reveal: You set the cyber unit on the students' networks and pre-empt 'instigators'. A few flashpoints are defused, and a generation learns that its every online word is watched, breeding the resentment that fuels the next eruption.

right.label: Leave student speech unmonitored.
right.tooltip: A government that surveils its students fears its future.
right.reveal: You decline to surveil the students. You lose an early-warning tool, but the young keep a space to argue and organise freely, and you are not the government that spied on its own schoolchildren.

speaker: Crime Investigation Chief
prompt: Cases against the powerful collapse because witnesses are bought, threatened, or killed, and there is no protection for those who dare testify. The chief wants a real witness-protection programme; it is costly, complex, and would make the untouchable suddenly prosecutable.

left.label: Build a witness-protection programme.
left.tooltip: A witness who can be reached is a verdict that can be bought.
left.reveal: You build genuine witness protection — relocation, security, anonymity. It is costly and hard to run, but suddenly cases against the powerful hold, and the era of the bought-off witness begins to end.

right.label: Leave witnesses to fend for themselves.
right.tooltip: A protection programme is a budget and a headache.
right.reveal: You decline the programme. The powerful keep buying and burying witnesses, the cases against them keep collapsing, and the brave who testify keep paying for it in blood or worse.

speaker: Border Guard Director
prompt: Your own border guards have shot dead unarmed villagers crossing for petty trade, mirroring the neighbour's notorious killings. The director asks whether to enforce strict non-lethal rules of engagement, or keep the shoot-to-deter posture that the brass prefers.

left.label: Enforce non-lethal rules of engagement.
left.tooltip: A guard who shoots a smuggler boy is no guard.
left.reveal: You impose strict non-lethal engagement and discipline the trigger-happy. The brass grumbles about a softer border, but the killings of unarmed villagers stop staining your own hands.

right.label: Keep the shoot-to-deter posture.
right.tooltip: A hard border is kept with a hard hand.
right.reveal: You leave the lethal posture in place. The border looks fierce, and your guards keep killing unarmed villagers over cattle and rice, handing the moral high ground and the headlines to your critics.

speaker: District Police Superintendent
prompt: A dense informant network in the slums would give you eyes on crime and dissent alike — and turn neighbour against neighbour, breeding fear and false accusations for petty grudges. The superintendent wants it built; the cost is the social fabric of the poorest places.

left.label: Build the slum informant network.
left.tooltip: Eyes in every alley miss nothing.
left.reveal: You weave informants through the slums. Crime intelligence improves, and so does the fear, as neighbours inform on neighbours and petty grudges become police cases and the poor learn to trust no one.

right.label: Rely on community policing instead.
right.tooltip: A neighbourhood of informers is a neighbourhood at war with itself.
right.reveal: You build trust-based community policing rather than an informant web. The intelligence is less granular, but the slums keep their fragile social fabric and the police gain genuine cooperation.

speaker: Surveillance Technology Director
prompt: New software can scan and identify every face at a rally, prayer gathering, or protest in real time against the national ID database. The director wants it live at all major gatherings; civil society warns it ends the anonymity that makes public assembly safe.

left.label: Scan every major gathering.
left.tooltip: A face in a crowd should not be a face I cannot name.
left.reveal: You deploy live facial scanning at gatherings. Every protester, worshipper, and marcher is now identifiable and loggable, and the chilling knowledge of it thins the crowds that dare to assemble.

right.label: Bar facial scanning of gatherings.
right.tooltip: A right to assemble means a right to assemble unnamed.
right.reveal: You forbid scanning peaceful gatherings. You forgo a powerful identification tool, but the anonymity that lets citizens protest and worship without fear of a logged face survives.

speaker: Prisons Medical Officer
prompt: Prisoners die of treatable illness for want of any real medical care in the jails, and the sick are ignored until they are corpses. The officer wants proper prison healthcare; it costs money for a constituency — convicts — that wins no votes and earns no sympathy.

left.label: Fund proper prison healthcare.
left.tooltip: A sentence is loss of liberty, not a death warrant.
left.reveal: You fund real medical care in the jails. There are no votes in it and the cost is real, but prisoners stop dying of treatable illness, and the state stops being a quiet executioner by neglect.

right.label: Leave prison healthcare to rot.
right.tooltip: A convict's health is the lowest claim on the budget.
right.reveal: You leave the jails without real medicine. The money goes to constituencies that vote, and prisoners keep dying of curable things, their deaths logged as misfortune and forgotten.

speaker: Rural Police Chief
prompt: A powerful local man is seizing a poor family's land by force, with hired muscle and a bought local police chief, and the family's appeals go nowhere. The chief asks whether to intervene against the powerful grabber or let the 'private dispute' run its course.

left.label: Intervene against the land-grabber.
left.tooltip: A poor man's deed is worth as much as a rich man's.
left.reveal: You send honest officers, protect the family, and move against the grabber and the bent local chief. The powerful man rages and pulls strings, but the family keeps its land and the law its meaning.

right.label: Treat it as a private dispute.
right.tooltip: I cannot referee every village land quarrel.
right.reveal: You stay out and call it a civil matter. The powerful man's muscle prevails, the family is dispossessed, and the lesson spreads that land here belongs to whoever can hire the most fists.

speaker: Hospital Liaison Officer
prompt: During unrest, police have been seizing injured protesters from hospital beds and pressuring doctors to deny them treatment or report them. The officer asks whether to guarantee medical neutrality — hospitals as sanctuaries — or let the security forces pursue the wounded inside.

left.label: Guarantee hospitals as neutral sanctuaries.
left.tooltip: A wounded man on a gurney is a patient, not a target.
left.reveal: You order hospitals off-limits to arrests and protect treating doctors. The security forces fume at the sanctuary, but the wounded are treated and the state is not seen dragging the bleeding from their beds.

right.label: Let security forces pursue the wounded.
right.tooltip: A hospital bed is no refuge from the law.
right.reveal: You let police seize the injured from the wards and lean on the doctors. The wounded learn that to seek treatment is to be arrested, and the images of patients hauled from gurneys travel the world.

speaker: Internal Affairs Director
prompt: A businessman who fell out with a powerful minister has vanished — picked up, witnesses say, by men in plainclothes. The trail leads uncomfortably close to your own circle. The director asks whether to investigate honestly or quietly close the file.

left.label: Investigate honestly, wherever it leads.
left.tooltip: A disappearance I refuse to solve is one I own.
left.reveal: You order a real investigation, and it points toward your own. The minister and the agencies close ranks against you, but the businessman's fate is uncovered and the rot, for once, is named.

right.label: Quietly close the file.
right.tooltip: Some threads are better not pulled.
right.reveal: You bury the investigation. The minister is protected and the agencies reassured, and another man simply ceases to exist, his family left to a silence the state enforces.

speaker: Internal Affairs Director
prompt: The case would not stay buried — a journalist has the witnesses, and the story is about to break regardless. The director asks whether to get ahead of it with a controlled admission and a scapegoat, or to crush the story and the journalist with it.

left.label: Crush the story and the journalist.
left.tooltip: What is never reported never happened.
left.reveal: You spike the story and threaten the journalist into silence. The suppression itself leaks, and now you own both the disappearance and the cover-up of the cover-up, a scandal compounding on itself.

right.label: Get ahead of it with an admission.
right.tooltip: A wound I lance heals; one I hide festers.
right.reveal: You concede the broad truth, offer a scapegoat and an inquiry, and free what can be freed. The base is unsettled and the agencies wary, but you blunt the story by meeting it rather than fleeing it.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: A term later, the same fault line tears open again. Students have filled the streets over the civil-service quota system — peaceful, enormous, and growing by the hour. The IGP wants permission to clear the intersections before they become permanent. "Give it a week," he warns, "and a road-block becomes a movement."

left.label: Authorise a forceful clearance now.
left.tooltip: A crowd unanswered only multiplies.
left.reveal: The batons and gas clear the roads for a day. The footage of bloodied students travels the world and triples the next crowd.

right.label: Hold the police back, open negotiations.
right.tooltip: You cannot tear-gas a generation into loving you.
right.reveal: You meet the student leaders. The cantonment frets that you flinched; the campuses, astonished, lower the temperature.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: Years into your rule, the matter you thought buried resurfaces — harder. Despite restraint, a video of a single unarmed student shot dead has become the symbol of the movement. The protest is no longer about quotas — it is about you. Your hardliners demand a curfew and an internet blackout.

left.label: Impose curfew and cut the internet.
left.tooltip: Silence the signal, scatter the crowd.
left.reveal: The blackout buys an eerie quiet and global condemnation. Businesses lose crores a day and the anger compounds in the dark.

right.label: Keep the internet on, hold an inquiry into the killing.
right.tooltip: A government that hides has already lost.
right.reveal: You name an inquiry and let the country watch. It is a gamble on transparency; the movement, given a hearing, begins to fracture between moderates and absolutists.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: A new season of power, an old problem with sharper teeth. The movement now demands the resignation of the ministers who ordered the violence — and the cantonment chief privately signals he will not order troops to fire on students again. The ground is shifting under your chair.

left.label: Sacrifice the hardline ministers.
left.tooltip: Better to lose a wing than the whole bird.
left.reveal: You dismiss the ministers blamed for the deaths. The crowd claims a scalp and pauses; your inner circle learns no one is safe.

right.label: Stand by your ministers, dig in.
right.tooltip: I will not feed my own people to a mob.
right.reveal: You refuse to bend and rally the loyal core. But with the army unwilling to shoot, your authority now rests on a crowd's patience.

speaker: Rights Commission Chair
prompt: The crisis returns, and this time the cabinet is watching. The Commission hands you a dossier: dozens vanished into the custody of your elite battalion, families turned away at every gate. "Some are alive in secret cells," the Chair says quietly. "You could bring them home. Or you could let the file gather dust like the last one."

left.label: Bury the dossier.
left.tooltip: The battalion is the spine of internal order.
left.reveal: The file vanishes and the cells stay full. The disappeared remain disappeared, and the rot spreads through the ranks that learn it is permitted.

right.label: Order the secret detainees released and investigated.
right.tooltip: A state that disappears its own has already fallen.
right.reveal: Survivors come home to weeping families and cameras. The battalion's commanders feel the betrayal; the world cautiously applauds.

speaker: Rights Commission Chair
prompt: Once more the issue lands on your desk, the stakes higher than before. With the cells emptying, a faction of officers — afraid of prosecution — floats a quiet warning through intermediaries: 'protect us with amnesty, or the force cannot guarantee its loyalty.' It is a polite mutiny.

left.label: Grant a quiet amnesty for past acts.
left.tooltip: Loyalty now outweighs justice later.
left.reveal: The officers stand down, reassured. Impunity is enshrined, and the families of the disappeared learn there will be no reckoning.

right.label: Refuse amnesty, reform the command instead.
right.tooltip: I will not be blackmailed by my own guns.
right.reveal: You retire the worst commanders and split the battalion's powers. It is dangerous — a wounded force is unpredictable — but the precedent of accountability holds.

speaker: ICT Enforcement Director
prompt: Another year survived, and the reckoning circles back. Under the cyber law, a cartoonist and two reporters sit in jail for posts mocking the first family. The Director offers more arrests under the same statute — "a few examples and the rest fall quiet" — or you could let the three out and blunt the law's teeth.

left.label: Make more examples under the Act.
left.tooltip: Mockery unchecked becomes contempt.
left.reveal: The arrests chill the newsrooms into caution. Editors self-censor, and 'press freedom' becomes the West's favourite stick to beat you with.

right.label: Free the three and amend the worst clauses.
right.tooltip: A government afraid of cartoons is afraid of itself.
right.reveal: The journalists walk free to grateful coverage. Your thin-skinned loyalists fume; your standing with the open world recovers.

speaker: District Magistrate, riot zone
prompt: The ground has shifted; the same demand arrives with new menace. During the Durga Puja, a rumour of a desecrated Quran has set mobs loose on Hindu temples and homes across several districts. The local police 'watched'. The Magistrate begs for orders before nightfall, when it will spread.

left.label: Deploy decisively and arrest the instigators.
left.tooltip: A pogrom on my watch is my disgrace.
left.reveal: Troops restore order overnight and the ringleaders are jailed. The hardline preachers who fanned it turn their sermons against you.

right.label: Issue a balanced statement, avoid 'taking sides'.
right.tooltip: Do not inflame the majority before an election.
right.reveal: You stay above it and let it burn out. The minorities learn their safety is negotiable; the cynics call it electoral arithmetic.

speaker: Cyber Intelligence Vendor
prompt: History rhymes: the conflict reopens deeper than last time. A foreign vendor offers a phone-hacking suite that can crack any device in the country — opposition, press, your own ministers. "Every modern state has one," the salesman murmurs. The price is steep and the oversight is whatever you decide it is.

left.label: Buy it and use it freely.
left.tooltip: Knowledge of every plot is worth the cost.
left.reveal: You gain a god's-eye view of your enemies — and your allies. When its existence leaks, as such things do, the scandal is radioactive.

right.label: Decline, or buy it under judicial warrant only.
right.tooltip: A tool with no leash is a leash on me.
right.reveal: You either pass or wrap it in court oversight. You lose some omniscience; you keep a state that can still call itself lawful.

speaker: Reform-minded Police Superintendent
prompt: Mid-reign, the old wound is torn afresh. A young SP requests an audience after a hawker dies in his thana's lock-up. "My OC tortured him over forty taka and wrote it as a heart attack. I can sign the false report and rise, or report my own station and be buried. I came to you to decide which."

left.label: Tell him to sign and let it lie.
left.tooltip: One hawker is not worth a war in the ranks.
left.reveal: The report is falsified and the SP, complicit now, climbs. Every constable learns the lock-up has no witnesses that matter.

right.label: Back him, prosecute the OC, protect the SP.
right.tooltip: A force that tortures the poor protects no one.
right.reveal: You make the honest officer a symbol and the OC an example. The old guard closes ranks against you; the public, briefly, believes in the police.

speaker: Camp Security Commander
prompt: The pressure you deferred has come due, larger now. The refugee mega-camps have become a black economy of yaba, trafficking, and rival armed gangs. The commander wants a hard 'cleansing' operation; the aid agencies warn it will be a bloodbath that the satellites will see.

left.label: Authorise the hard operation.
left.tooltip: A lawless camp threatens the whole coast.
left.reveal: The gangs are crushed and the camp goes quiet. Civilian casualties and footage of the sweep hand your critics a year of ammunition.

right.label: Build a vetted camp police with the agencies.
right.tooltip: Order earned beats order imposed.
right.reveal: You stand up a joint, accountable force. It is slow and imperfect, but the worst gangs lose their grip without a massacre.

speaker: Special Branch Director
prompt: A later term, and the quarrel has hardened into a test. With the vote months away, the Special Branch proposes a sweep: mass cases against opposition organisers to 'thin their machine' before the campaign. "Empty their streets now," the Director says, "and the result handles itself."

left.label: Greenlight the pre-election sweep.
left.tooltip: A contest you control is a contest you win.
left.reveal: Thousands of organisers are jailed on old cases. The opposition cries foul to a sympathetic world; the result is yours and tainted.

right.label: Forbid it, let them campaign.
right.tooltip: A win that needs empty jails is a loss in disguise.
right.reveal: You let the opposition march. The contest is real and dangerous; if you win, you win something worth holding.

speaker: Anti-Narcotics Director
prompt: The country has changed, but this fault line has not — only worsened. Methamphetamine pours over the eastern border and floods the slums. The director proposes a hardline 'war on drugs' — sweeping arrests and a quiet tolerance for crossfire deaths of suspected dealers.

left.label: Launch the war on drugs.
left.tooltip: Burn the trade out, root and branch.
left.reveal: The headlines blaze with seizures. The 'crossfire' bodies pile up, many of them poor and unproven, and the rights world recoils.

right.label: Treat addiction as health, target the kingpins.
right.tooltip: You cannot shoot your way out of a chemical.
right.reveal: You build clinics and chase the financiers instead. It is slow and unglamorous; the slums, in time, breathe easier.

speaker: Road Safety Movement
prompt: It returns, as these things do, with the patience gone. Two students are crushed by a reckless bus and the city's schoolchildren pour into the streets, checking licences and shaming the state's rotten road system. The traffic — and your authority — is paralysed.

left.label: Concede: pass a real road-safety law.
left.tooltip: The children are policing what we would not.
left.reveal: You meet their demands and jail the worst operators. The transport-owner lobby, thick with your men, is enraged.

right.label: Clear the streets and brand it a plot.
right.tooltip: Schoolchildren do not run my roads.
right.reveal: Your cadres and police break the movement. The images of beaten students travel the world and stain the year.

speaker: District Police Super
prompt: Years on, the matter is no longer private; the press is already circling. A WhatsApp rumour of child-snatchers has set off a wave of mob lynchings; innocent travellers are being beaten to death in village squares. The SP wants emergency powers and an internet throttle.

left.label: Throttle the rumour networks and flood police out.
left.tooltip: Silence the lie, save the lives.
left.reveal: The lynchings ebb as connectivity drops and patrols rise. Businesses and rights groups protest the heavy throttle.

right.label: Run a public-truth campaign, no shutdown.
right.tooltip: You fight a rumour with the truth, not a blackout.
right.reveal: You blanket the airwaves with the facts and arrest ringleaders. Slower to bite, but the open net is preserved.

speaker: Hill Tracts Commander
prompt: The same knot, retied tighter, lands before you once more. The decades-old Hill Tracts accord is fraying; an armed indigenous faction ambushes a patrol while Bengali settlers demand protection and more land. The commander wants a hard sweep of the hills.

left.label: Order the military sweep.
left.tooltip: Restore the writ of the state in the hills.
left.reveal: The faction scatters under pressure. Indigenous villages report abuses, settlers push deeper, and the grievance hardens.

right.label: Revive the accord and freeze settlement.
right.tooltip: The hills were promised autonomy; honour it.
right.reveal: You restart talks and halt the land grab. The settler lobby and some generals seethe; the hills, warily, quiet.

speaker: Women's Rights Commission
prompt: Another monsoon, another return of an argument you cannot end. A horrific assault, filmed and shared, has the country marching for justice and the death penalty for rape. The commission wants real reform — fast courts, victim protection — not just a hanging law.

left.label: Pass capital punishment and call it done.
left.tooltip: Give the street the sentence it demands.
left.reveal: The crowd is sated by the gallows clause. Conviction rates barely move; the deeper rot in policing and courts is untouched.

right.label: Build fast-track courts and victim protection.
right.tooltip: A noose is not a justice system.
right.reveal: You fund the slow machinery of real protection. Less cathartic, but conviction and reporting finally begin to climb.

speaker: Anti-Trafficking Chief
prompt: Deep into the reign, the issue demands a final-sounding answer. Smugglers sell desperate young men a deadly dream — boats to Europe via Libya, where many drown or are ransomed. The trade runs through brokers in your own districts who pay local police.

left.label: Smash the broker networks and the dirty police.
left.tooltip: We bury our youth in the Mediterranean.
left.reveal: You jail brokers and the officers on their payroll. The trade fragments; the bought policemen and their patrons turn on you.

right.label: Look away; the remittances and the relief valve matter.
right.tooltip: Desperate men will go regardless.
right.reveal: The boats keep sailing and the brokers keep paying. Every drowned son's family learns the state shrugged.

speaker: Prisons Inspector-General
prompt: The reckoning you postponed has matured into a crisis. The jails hold three times their capacity, many of them opposition men on old cases never tried. The inspector warns of a tuberculosis outbreak and a riot brewing in the central jail.

left.label: Release the untried on bail to ease the jails.
left.tooltip: A prison of the un-charged is a scandal.
left.reveal: Thousands walk free pending trial; the jails decompress. Your hardliners call it surrender to the opposition.

right.label: Hold the line; build more cells.
right.tooltip: The streets are safer with them inside.
right.reveal: You keep the jails full and pour money into more bars. The outbreak spreads, and the world counts your political prisoners.

speaker: Fire Service Director
prompt: A new chapter, the same antagonists, less room to manoeuvre. A chemical warehouse in a packed old-city lane ignites and the death toll climbs past a hundred. The director wants every illegal warehouse and unsafe high-rise shut — owned, mostly, by the connected.

left.label: Shut the death-trap buildings citywide.
left.tooltip: No more pyres in the old city.
left.reveal: Inspectors padlock hundreds of illegal premises. Commerce screams and powerful landlords mobilise against the crackdown.

right.label: Fine them and let them reopen.
right.tooltip: You cannot empty half the old city.
right.reveal: A few fines, a press conference, and the lanes reopen as before. The next fire is already being stacked, drum by drum.

speaker: Immigration Director
prompt: The old fault line widens; this time there is no quiet option. Getting a passport means paying a broker who splits the bribe with your own officers; the honest applicant waits months. The director offers a fully digital, broker-proof system — and a revolt from the staff who feed on the racket.

left.label: Force the digital, broker-proof rollout.
left.tooltip: A passport is a right, not a bribe.
left.reveal: The brokers lose their grip and applicants their agony. The displaced officers sabotage and slander the new system.

right.label: Keep the human counters and the side-economy.
right.tooltip: Do not provoke the whole department at once.
right.reveal: The counters stay, and so does the toll on every citizen. The racket hums on under your seal.

speaker: VIP Protocol Officer
prompt: Time has not healed it — the conflict comes back armed. A minister's speeding convoy killed a rickshaw-puller and an ambulance idled behind the cavalcade as a patient died. The footage is everywhere; the protocol officer asks how to handle the minister.

left.label: Discipline the minister, curb VIP convoys.
left.tooltip: No siren outruns an ambulance.
left.reveal: You publicly clip the privilege and the minister. The cabinet's grandees fume at the humiliation; the street cheers.

right.label: Protect the minister, manage the press.
right.tooltip: One does not feed a colleague to a mob.
right.reveal: The story is smothered and the convoys roll on. The dead man's family, and the watching city, file it away.

speaker: NID Database Chief
prompt: Once again, and with the whole house holding its breath. The national ID database — fingerprints, faces, the whole population — could be wired to a live surveillance grid. The chief says it would find any dissident in minutes. It would also find anyone, anytime.

left.label: Build the live surveillance grid.
left.tooltip: A state that sees all fears nothing.
left.reveal: You gain a near-omniscient eye over the country. When its reach leaks, the outrage at home and abroad is volcanic.

right.label: Wall the database behind strict warrants.
right.tooltip: A tool that watches everyone is a chain on everyone.
right.reveal: You keep the ID system civil, not panoptic. You lose an instrument of control and keep a shred of liberty.

speaker: Cyber Crime Unit Head
prompt: The matter resurfaces in a harder year, patience exhausted. Online betting rings and OTP-fraud gangs are fleecing the public through foreign-hosted sites, and some local agents are tied to party youth. The head wants a sweeping cyber-crackdown.

left.label: Crack down hard, agents and all.
left.tooltip: Cut the scam off at the local hand.
left.reveal: Arrests roll up the fraud rings and the party-linked agents both. A slice of your youth wing is suddenly facing court.

right.label: Chase the foreign sites, spare the local agents.
right.tooltip: Do not jail our own boys over a side hustle.
right.reveal: You block sites and spare the agents. The fraud reroutes and the public keeps bleeding small sums into the dark.

speaker: Police Recruitment Board
prompt: A later season of rule, the stakes now openly existential. Constable jobs are openly sold for lakhs, so the force fills with men in debt to the brokers who placed them — and primed to extort to repay. The board offers a transparent, merit-only recruitment.

left.label: Enforce merit-only recruitment.
left.tooltip: A bought constable polices for his broker.
left.reveal: Honest recruits enter a cleaner force. The recruitment mafias and the local bosses who ran them lose a fortune and turn hostile.

right.label: Keep the comfortable status quo.
right.tooltip: Do not war with every thana at once.
right.reveal: The jobs keep selling and the new constables keep extorting to clear their debts. The rot renews itself.

speaker: Transport Authority Chief
prompt: It comes around again, and the margin for error is gone. The capital is one vast jam of unfit, unlicensed vehicles run by syndicates that pay everyone. The chief wants to scrap the death-trap buses — owned by men who can put thousands of strikers on the road.

left.label: Scrap the unfit fleets.
left.tooltip: A city cannot run on rolling coffins.
left.reveal: Dangerous buses come off the road and the air clears a little. The transport syndicates shut the city down in retaliation.

right.label: Negotiate softly with the syndicates.
right.tooltip: They can paralyse the capital in an hour.
right.reveal: You trade enforcement for peace with the bosses. The coffins keep rolling and the gridlock thickens.

speaker: Counter-Terror Chief
prompt: The same demand, but the reign is older and the knives sharper. After a café siege shocked the world, the chief has cells under watch and wants pre-emptive raids. Go too hard and you radicalise the pious mainstream; go too soft and the next attack is on you.

left.label: Authorise aggressive pre-emptive raids.
left.tooltip: Strike the cells before they strike us.
left.reveal: The plots are disrupted and the world credits your resolve. Heavy-handed raids on the wrong doors breed quiet new grievances.

right.label: Pair surgical policing with de-radicalisation.
right.tooltip: You cannot raid a sermon out of a mind.
right.reveal: You blend precise arrests with community work. Slower reassurance for the West, but a thinner recruiting ground over time.

speaker: Slum Eviction Magistrate
prompt: Years deep, the unresolved returns to be resolved for good. A vast riverside slum sits on prime state land a developer covets, and the magistrate has bulldozers ready. Half a million of the city's poorest — your voters — live in those tin lanes.

left.label: Send in the bulldozers for the developer.
left.tooltip: The land is worth more than the shanties.
left.reveal: The towers will rise and the treasury will profit. Half a million are scattered into the monsoon, and they remember your name.

right.label: Halt the eviction, regularise the settlement.
right.tooltip: You do not bulldoze your own people for a tower.
right.reveal: You give the slum legal footing and basic services. The developer and his patrons lose a fortune and your favour.

speaker: Special Branch Analyst
prompt: The conflict reignites, and the world is watching this time. The branch has assembled a watchlist of journalists, academics, and activists — 'persons of interest'. The analyst wants authority to tap, trail, and pressure them pre-emptively.

left.label: Authorise the surveillance dragnet.
left.tooltip: Know your critics before they move.
left.reveal: You blind-side dissent before it organises. When the watchlist leaks, every name becomes a martyr and a headline.

right.label: Disband the watchlist.
right.tooltip: A state afraid of its scholars is already weak.
right.reveal: You stand down the dragnet. You lose an early-warning net and keep the country a place thought can still be spoken.

speaker: Border Guard Sector Commander
prompt: A familiar crisis, escalated by everything you let slide. Cattle, phensedyl, and gold flow across the porous border through villages that live on the trade, while the neighbour's force shoots the smugglers dead. The commander wants legal border-haats to replace the black market.

left.label: Open legal border markets.
left.tooltip: Trade in daylight beats trade under fire.
left.reveal: Sanctioned haats pull commerce into the open and cut the killings. The smuggling syndicates lose their margins and snarl.

right.label: Crack down hard on the border villages.
right.tooltip: Smuggling is smuggling; stamp it out.
right.reveal: Raids choke the trade and the border economy collapses. The villages, robbed of their living, curse the state that 'saved' them.

speaker: Riot Control Commander
prompt: The fault line cracks wider; the decision can no longer wait. A grand opposition rally has filled the central avenue, peaceful but immense. The commander's finger hovers over the order: disperse it now with gas and batons, or let the sea of people have its day.

left.label: Disperse the rally by force.
left.tooltip: A crowd that size is a threat, not a meeting.
left.reveal: The avenue is cleared in a haze of gas and blood. The footage hardens the opposition and shames you abroad.

right.label: Police it lightly and let it pass.
right.tooltip: Let them shout; tomorrow they go home.
right.reveal: The rally swells, peaks, and disperses on its own. Your hawks call it weakness; the day passes without a martyr.

speaker: Disappearances Liaison
prompt: Late in the reign, the old question demands a heavier answer. Mothers of the vanished hold a silent vigil that has become a weekly fixture and a global symbol. A UN body wants to send investigators. Your security men want them refused at the airport.

left.label: Admit the investigators and open the cases.
left.tooltip: A state that hides its vans has lost its soul.
left.reveal: Some of the missing surface; the world cautiously applauds the candour. The agencies responsible feel the ground shift beneath them.

right.label: Refuse the UN team and disperse the mothers.
right.tooltip: No foreigner audits our security.
right.reveal: You bar the investigators and clear the vigil. The mothers' grief becomes the opposition's sharpest, most enduring weapon.

speaker: Domestic Worker Advocate
prompt: It returns once more — and this round may be the one remembered. A child maid is found tortured in a wealthy home — not the first. The advocate wants registration and inspection of domestic workers, exposing what the comfortable classes do behind their gates.

left.label: Regulate and inspect domestic work.
left.tooltip: No child should vanish behind a rich gate.
left.reveal: Inspections begin to pierce the private cruelty. The affluent — many of them your supporters — resent the intrusion.

right.label: Treat it as a private family matter.
right.tooltip: The state does not enter the drawing room.
right.reveal: You leave the gates closed and the maids unseen. The next tortured child is another headline you will have to manage.

speaker: Election Security Chief
prompt: The reckoning circles back, grown teeth in the waiting. Polling day looms and rival cadres are arming for the usual battle over the centres. The chief can deploy the army neutrally to guarantee a clean vote — or deploy it where it quietly helps your side.

left.label: Deploy the army strictly neutrally.
left.tooltip: Let the count be clean or not at all.
left.reveal: The vote passes with rare calm and credibility. Your own machine grumbles that you gave away the home advantage.

right.label: Tilt the deployment to your strongholds.
right.tooltip: Security is also strategy.
right.reveal: Your centres are safe; the opposition's are not. You bank the seats and the observers' damning footnotes both.

speaker: Acid & Dowry Cell
prompt: Another term, the same antagonist, the stakes raised again. Acid attacks have ticked up again with the price of revenge gone cheap. The cell wants tight control of open acid sales and special tribunals — opposed by the chemical traders' lobby.

left.label: Lock down acid sales, fast-track the cases.
left.tooltip: A face burned for a dowry is a state's failure.
left.reveal: Open acid vanishes from the bazaars and convictions quicken. The chemical traders lobby and grumble, but the wards see fewer victims.

right.label: Leave the trade lightly regulated.
right.tooltip: Do not strangle a whole trade over a few cases.
right.reveal: Acid stays cheap and available. Another disfigured woman, another vigil, another file you will answer for.

speaker: Crowd Safety Officer
