prompt: The matter, long deferred, lands with the force of a verdict. A free-rice distribution and a religious gathering both expect vast crowds the same week, and last year a stampede killed dozens. The officer wants strict crowd-control and fewer, ticketed events.

left.label: Impose strict crowd controls.
left.tooltip: I will not preside over another stampede.
left.reveal: The events run safely under tight management. Some call the controls heavy-handed and the charity less generous.

right.label: Keep the events open and unmanaged.
right.tooltip: Do not fence in the people's gatherings.
right.reveal: The crowds flow free and vast. The risk you waved away is one bad surge from becoming your next tragedy.

speaker: Counter-Terror Commander
prompt: Years on and unhealed, the conflict returns to claim its answer. Militants have stormed an upscale café full of foreigners and taken hostages, executing those who cannot recite scripture. The world is watching live. The commander can storm it now with heavy force, or negotiate and wait, each hour risking more throats.

left.label: Storm the café immediately.
left.tooltip: Every hour of delay is another execution.
left.reveal: Commandos breach and end the siege in blood. Some hostages die in the assault, but the militants are wiped out and the state shows it will not be held to ransom.

right.label: Negotiate and wait for a clean rescue.
right.tooltip: A panicked assault fills the morgue.
right.reveal: You hold off and work for a safer resolution. The standoff drags agonisingly and the watching world frets, but a patient rescue may yet save those still alive inside.

speaker: Counter-Terror Commander
prompt: The same fault line, the harder hour, the narrower path. The siege is over, and the attackers turn out to be not impoverished fanatics but privileged, educated young men from good families — a fact that terrifies the elite. The commander wants a sweeping crackdown on suspect youth; the alternative is a quieter de-radicalisation drive.

left.label: Launch a sweeping crackdown on suspect youth.
left.tooltip: Root out the rot before it spreads.
left.reveal: Mass detentions sweep up the young and the bearded. A few cells are broken, but the dragnet radicalises as many as it catches, and the abuses pile up.

right.label: Invest in quiet de-radicalisation.
right.tooltip: You cannot arrest your way out of an idea.
right.reveal: You build counselling, monitoring, and community programmes instead of mass arrests. The results are slow and invisible, but the pipeline of recruits begins, quietly, to thin.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: It comes back sharpened by every season you survived. A rumour on social media that strangers were kidnapping children for a bridge's 'human sacrifice' has triggered a wave of mob lynchings — innocent people beaten to death on suspicion. The IGP wants a hard order to fire on lynch mobs; the alternative is a rumour-control and arrest drive.

left.label: Authorise firing on lynch mobs.
left.tooltip: A mob that kills must fear the gun.
left.reveal: Police open fire to break the worst mobs and the lynchings ebb. The deaths at police hands draw their own outcry, and the underlying panic still smoulders online.

right.label: Crack down on rumours and arrest ringleaders.
right.tooltip: Kill the lie and the mob dissolves.
right.reveal: You flood the networks with truth, arrest instigators, and protect the accused. It is slower than bullets, but the panic deflates without the state adding to the body count.

speaker: Home Secretary
prompt: A later year of power, the old crisis now a true test of it. A vast Islamist movement has occupied the heart of the capital, demanding a blasphemy law and the removal of a 'pagan' sculpture, refusing to leave. The secretary offers a midnight clearance by force, or patient negotiation that concedes some symbolic ground.

left.label: Clear the square by force at night.
left.tooltip: The capital cannot be held hostage.
left.reveal: Police and paramilitaries clear the square in a dark, bloody operation. The occupation ends and rumours of a massacre spread; the movement gains martyrs and a grievance that will not die.

right.label: Negotiate and concede symbolic ground.
right.tooltip: A square emptied by talk leaves no martyrs.
right.reveal: You move the disputed sculpture and promise to 'study' their demands, and the occupation disperses. Secularists feel betrayed by the concession, but the capital is spared a massacre.

speaker: Intelligence Procurement Chief
prompt: The unresolved returns, and this time it will not be deferred. A foreign vendor offers military-grade phone spyware that cracks any device — a tool to read every plot, journalist, and judge in the country. The chief is desperate for it; the catch is the inevitable leak that you bought a weapon to spy on your own people.

left.label: Buy the spyware.
left.tooltip: A leader who cannot read the room is blind.
left.reveal: You acquire the spyware and the secrets pour in. Every rival's phone is an open book — until the purchase leaks, and the word 'surveillance state' attaches to your name for good.

right.label: Decline the spyware.
right.tooltip: A tool that reads everyone eventually reads its master's enemies into power.
right.reveal: You pass on the spyware. You forgo a formidable surveillance edge, but you are spared the day an exposé reveals you bugged judges, journalists, and your own ministers.

speaker: Prisons Inspector-General
prompt: A term later, the same fault line tears open again. The jails hold three times their capacity, swollen with opposition activists swept up in mass cases. Disease and deaths in custody mount. The IG can release the low-level political detainees to ease the crush, or hold them all and build more cells.

left.label: Hold them all, build more cells.
left.tooltip: A freed activist is a refilled street.
left.reveal: You keep the jails packed and order new prisons built. The opposition stays caged and the streets quiet, but the deaths in custody and the cost of the cells both keep climbing.

right.label: Release the low-level political detainees.
right.tooltip: A jail bursting with the innocent shames the state.
right.reveal: You free thousands held on flimsy mass cases. The hardliners warn the streets will fill, but the custodial deaths fall and the courts and jails can breathe.

speaker: University Affairs Officer
prompt: Years into your rule, the matter you thought buried resurfaces — harder. Members of your party's student wing have tortured a student to death in a dorm 'guest room' for a Facebook post criticising the government. The campus and the nation are erupting. The officer asks whether to shield the cadres or hand them to justice.

left.label: Shield the student-wing cadres.
left.tooltip: I do not feed my own loyalists to a mob.
left.reveal: You quietly protect the killers and float counter-narratives. The cover-up enrages the campuses further, and the dead student becomes a permanent symbol of your wing's impunity.

right.label: Hand the killers to justice.
right.tooltip: A boy tortured to death is not a loyalty test.
right.reveal: You let the cadres be arrested and charged. The student wing feels betrayed and the machine grumbles, but the campuses, stunned, see a flicker of accountability.

speaker: Smart City Director
prompt: A new season of power, an old problem with sharper teeth. A 'safe city' project would blanket the capital in cameras with live facial recognition — a genuine boon against crime and terror, and a perfect tool for tracking every protester and dissident. The director wants the full system; civil society is alarmed.

left.label: Deploy the full facial-recognition net.
left.tooltip: A watched city is a safe city.
left.reveal: Cameras and face-tracking blanket the capital. Street crime and terror plots get easier to foil, and so does following every activist from home to rally to home.

right.label: Deploy cameras without facial tracking.
right.tooltip: I will deter the mugger without indexing the marcher.
right.reveal: You install the cameras but bar the face-recognition layer. You gain real crime deterrence without building a tool to map every dissident's movements.

speaker: Security Reform Adviser
prompt: The crisis returns, and this time the cabinet is watching. Your elite battalion is under foreign sanctions for extrajudicial killings, and the crossfire deaths have all but stopped under scrutiny — but crime is creeping back and hardliners want the gloves off again. The adviser asks whether to entrench the reform or quietly revert.

left.label: Quietly let the crossfire killings resume.
left.tooltip: Fear was the only law the criminals respected.
left.reveal: You ease the leash and the 'crossfire' deaths quietly return. Crime dips and the hardliners cheer, but the sanctions harden and the reform's brief promise dies.

right.label: Entrench the reform, rebuild on accountability.
right.tooltip: A force that murders cannot be trusted to police.
right.reveal: You lock in the restraint and rebuild the battalion around oversight and proper investigation. Crime-fighting is slower and the hardliners scornful, but the sanctions ease and a killing machine becomes a police force.

speaker: Police Reform Commissioner
prompt: Once more the issue lands on your desk, the stakes higher than before. At the local police stations, nothing moves without a bribe — filing a case, getting bail, even reporting a crime. The commissioner wants a transparency overhaul with citizen oversight and digital records; the rank-and-file, who live on the bribes, will resist fiercely.

left.label: Force the transparency overhaul.
left.tooltip: A police that sells justice is a syndicate in uniform.
left.reveal: You impose digital records, citizen oversight, and anti-bribery teams. The rank-and-file seethe and slow-walk, but ordinary people start getting served without paying for it.

right.label: Leave the thana culture alone.
right.tooltip: A demoralised force is a useless one.
right.reveal: You decline to disturb the station-house economy. The constables stay content and pliable, and the poor keep paying a bribe for every scrap of justice the state owes them free.

speaker: Anti-Trafficking Chief
prompt: Another year survived, and the reckoning circles back. A trafficking syndicate is loading desperate young men onto death-trap boats for the sea route to Malaysia, where many drown or end in jungle camps — and the network has police and border-guard protection. The chief wants it smashed, names and all.

left.label: Smash the network, protectors and all.
left.tooltip: A trade in drowning men cannot have a uniform's blessing.
left.reveal: You break the syndicate and expose the officers shielding it. The trafficking route is disrupted and the corrupt purged, to international relief and some internal fury.

right.label: Take down only the visible smugglers.
right.tooltip: Some uniforms are better left unexamined.
right.reveal: You arrest the front-line smugglers and leave their protectors untouched. The route reopens under new management within weeks, and the boats keep sailing into the dark.

speaker: Religious Harmony Officer
prompt: The ground has shifted; the same demand arrives with new menace. A hardline movement is whipping up mobs against a small heterodox Muslim sect, attacking their mosques and demanding they be declared non-Muslim. The officer asks whether to protect the sect with force, or appease the majority by quietly conceding the demand.

left.label: Protect the sect with force.
left.tooltip: The state shields the few from the many, or it shields no one.
left.reveal: You deploy security to defend the sect's mosques and arrest the instigators. The hardline preachers turn their fury on you, but the constitution's promise of equal protection holds.

right.label: Appease the majority, concede the demand.
right.tooltip: I will not burn the country for one small sect.
right.reveal: You quietly distance the state from the sect to placate the mob. The hardliners are triumphant, the violence subsides, and a small community learns the state will trade them for peace.

speaker: Cultural Affairs Officer
prompt: History rhymes: the conflict reopens deeper than last time. The great secular new-year festival — music, masks, processions — faces a credible militant bomb threat, and hardliners are also denouncing it as 'un-Islamic'. The officer can lock it down behind a security wall, or cancel it 'for safety' and quietly please the zealots.

left.label: Hold the festival behind heavy security.
left.tooltip: Our culture will not be cancelled by a threat.
left.reveal: You ring the festival with security and it goes ahead, defiant and joyous. The cost and the tension are high, but the secular tradition stands and the militants are denied their veto.

right.label: Cancel the festival 'for safety'.
right.tooltip: A dead crowd is too high a price for a parade.
right.reveal: You call it off, citing the threat. The zealots quietly celebrate the cancellation they wanted, the secularists mourn a tradition surrendered, and the militants learn that a threat is enough.

speaker: Internal Affairs Director
prompt: Mid-reign, the old wound is torn afresh. A serving officer has leaked documents proving a senior commander ordered a string of fake 'crossfire' killings. The director can prosecute the whistleblower for breaching secrecy and bury the revelation, or protect him and pursue the commander.

left.label: Prosecute the whistleblower, bury it.
left.tooltip: A leaker is a traitor whatever he reveals.
left.reveal: You jail the whistleblower and quash the story. The killings stay covered, but the lesson lands in every honest officer: speak, and the state will break you, not the killer.

right.label: Protect him, pursue the commander.
right.tooltip: An officer with a conscience is worth ten without.
right.reveal: You shield the whistleblower and move against the commander. The old guard is shaken and warns of low morale, but a culture of impunity takes its first real blow.

speaker: District Magistrate
prompt: The pressure you deferred has come due, larger now. The opposition has called a massive rally in the capital, and you can pre-emptively ban all gatherings under the old colonial assembly law, choking it before it starts. The magistrate warns that the ban itself may provoke exactly the confrontation you fear.

left.label: Ban the rally under Section 144.
left.tooltip: A rally forbidden is a rally that cannot grow.
left.reveal: You blanket the city in an assembly ban and flood it with police. The rally is throttled, but the heavy-handed lockdown of the capital becomes the story, and the grievance hardens.

right.label: Permit the rally under conditions.
right.tooltip: A banned gathering becomes a battle; a permitted one becomes a speech.
right.reveal: You allow the rally within agreed limits. The opposition gets its day and its crowd, but the feared confrontation never comes, and the state looks confident rather than afraid.

speaker: Arms Licensing Officer
prompt: A later term, and the quarrel has hardened into a test. Local party leaders are pressing for a wave of private firearms licences — ostensibly for self-defence, in truth to arm the local muscle that wins elections. The officer can approve the lot as a loyalty reward, or tighten licensing and disarm the cadres.

left.label: Approve the firearms licences.
left.tooltip: An armed friend is a loyal friend.
left.reveal: You sign off on the licences and the local strongmen are armed. They deliver elections and intimidate rivals, and the country grows a little more lawless, district by district.

right.label: Tighten licensing, disarm the cadres.
right.tooltip: A party with guns is a militia in waiting.
right.reveal: You clamp down on private firearms, your own side included. The local leaders feel disarmed and betrayed, but the cycle of armed political muscle loosens its grip.

speaker: Counter-Terror Commander
prompt: The country has changed, but this fault line has not — only worsened. A handful of citizens who went abroad to fight for a terror group want to return, some with wives and children born in the war zone. The commander can bar or jail them all as a danger, or repatriate and rehabilitate under close watch.

left.label: Bar or jail the returnees outright.
left.tooltip: A trained jihadi is a threat, not a project.
left.reveal: You refuse entry or jail those who slip back. The hard line plays well, but the stranded radicalise further abroad and the children pay for their fathers' choices, drawing rights criticism.

right.label: Repatriate and rehabilitate under watch.
right.tooltip: A child of the caliphate is still a child.
right.reveal: You bring them home into monitoring and de-radicalisation, prosecuting the guilty. It is a risk and the public is uneasy, but you reclaim the salvageable and the innocent rather than abandoning them.

speaker: Information Security Officer
prompt: It returns, as these things do, with the patience gone. You can quietly strip parliamentary and secretariat press accreditation from the most critical journalists — cutting their access without an arrest. The officer calls it clean and deniable; it is also an unmistakable signal that coverage is now conditional on loyalty.

left.label: Strip the critics' accreditation.
left.tooltip: Access is a privilege for the friendly.
left.reveal: The critical reporters lose their passes and their scoops. The press corps gets the message and softens, and 'managed media' becomes one more charge in the indictment of your rule.

right.label: Keep accreditation open to all.
right.tooltip: A press corps of flatterers tells me nothing.
right.reveal: You leave access untouched, critics and all. The tough questions keep coming at every briefing, but your government keeps a free press it can point to abroad.

speaker: Prisons Inspector-General
prompt: Years on, the matter is no longer private; the press is already circling. Convicted militants have broken out of a high-security prison with apparent inside help, and are loose in the country. The IG wants a massive, disruptive nationwide manhunt with checkpoints and detentions; the alternative is a targeted, intelligence-led pursuit.

left.label: Launch a massive nationwide dragnet.
left.tooltip: Flood the country until they surface.
left.reveal: Checkpoints and sweeps blanket the nation. The disruption is enormous and the wrongful detentions many, and the fugitives may still slip the clumsy net.

right.label: Run a targeted intelligence-led pursuit.
right.tooltip: A scalpel catches what a sledgehammer scatters.
right.reveal: You hunt the escapees quietly through intelligence and informants. It is slower and the public nervous, but the country is not paralysed and the inside helpers are traced too.

speaker: Industrial Police Chief
prompt: The same knot, retied tighter, lands before you once more. Tens of thousands of garment workers have walked out over unpaid wages and blocked the industrial highways. The factory owners — your donors — demand the protest crushed; the chief can send the industrial police in hard, or broker a wage settlement instead.

left.label: Crush the worker protest.
left.tooltip: The export zones cannot be held hostage.
left.reveal: The police clear the highways with batons and tear gas, and a worker or two die. The owners are grateful and the lines restart, but the deaths become a global stain on the garment brand.

right.label: Broker a wage settlement.
right.tooltip: Unpaid workers are a fire; do not fan it with batons.
right.reveal: You force the owners to pay the arrears and mediate a raise. The donors grumble at the squeeze, but the highways clear peacefully and the workers go back with their dignity.

speaker: Land Records Commissioner
prompt: Another monsoon, another return of an argument you cannot end. Under a notorious old law, minority Hindu families' land has been seized as 'enemy property' and grabbed by the locally powerful — often your own people. A restitution drive could return it; the grabbers, with party ties, will fight to keep their spoils.

left.label: Drive the land restitution.
left.tooltip: Land stolen under a bigot's law must go home.
left.reveal: You push to return the seized property to its rightful owners. The connected grabbers rage and resist parcel by parcel, but a historic injustice begins, at last, to be undone.

right.label: Leave the seized land with its holders.
right.tooltip: I will not evict my own base for old ghosts.
right.reveal: You let the grabbers keep their spoils. Your local power-holders are secured, and the minority families learn once more that the law that robbed them will not give it back.

speaker: NID Database Director
prompt: Deep into the reign, the issue demands a final-sounding answer. The national ID system can be linked to phones, banks, travel, and biometrics into a single all-seeing profile of every citizen — efficient government, and a surveillance dossier on the entire population. The director wants full integration; privacy advocates are aghast.

left.label: Build the fully integrated profile.
left.tooltip: A state that knows its citizens governs them well.
left.reveal: You fuse the databases into one omniscient citizen profile. Services get slicker and fraud harder, and the state acquires a dossier on every life that no future ruler will ever dismantle.

right.label: Keep the databases walled off.
right.tooltip: A single key to every life is too much for any hand.
right.reveal: You bar the full integration and keep firewalls between the systems. Government is clunkier and some fraud slips through, but no all-seeing profile of the citizenry is ever assembled.

speaker: Education-Security Liaison
prompt: The reckoning you postponed has matured into a crisis. Tens of thousands of unregistered religious schools teach a curriculum the state cannot see, some shading into extremism. The liaison wants them registered and made to teach maths, science, and citizenship; the clerical establishment calls it an assault on faith.

left.label: Force registration and a modern curriculum.
left.tooltip: A school the state cannot see is a school it cannot trust.
left.reveal: You bring the madrasas under registration and a broadened curriculum. The clerics protest the intrusion and some resist, but a generation gains numeracy and a window onto the wider world.

right.label: Leave the religious schools autonomous.
right.tooltip: I will not pick a fight with every mosque in the land.
right.reveal: You leave the madrasas to themselves to keep the clergy onside. The establishment is grateful, but a vast parallel education stays beyond the state's sight, curriculum and all.

speaker: Traffic Division Chief
prompt: A new chapter, the same antagonists, less room to manoeuvre. The traffic police live off a daily tax on every bus, truck, and rickshaw, waving through the dangerous and fining the compliant. The chief can launch a clean-up with cashless fines and oversight, or leave the lucrative racket that keeps the force quietly content.

left.label: Clean up the traffic racket.
left.tooltip: A road tax in a constable's pocket is theft with a whistle.
left.reveal: You bring in cashless fines, cameras, and oversight. The constables lose their daily skim and resent it, but the roads grow fractionally fairer and safer.

right.label: Leave the traffic police to their ways.
right.tooltip: A contented constable is a compliant one.
right.reveal: You leave the roadside economy alone. The force stays loyal and well-fed on bribes, and the buses that pay keep killing on the highways while the honest pay the fines.

speaker: Counter-Terror Commander
prompt: The old fault line widens; this time there is no quiet option. Militants are bombing and hacking to death the keepers of Sufi shrines — the gentle, syncretic Islam the hardliners despise. The commander wants visible armed protection at hundreds of shrines and a crackdown on the preachers who incite it; the latter means confronting powerful clerics.

left.label: Protect the shrines and confront the inciters.
left.tooltip: The faith of the saints will not be bombed into silence.
left.reveal: You guard the shrines and move against the preachers fuelling the attacks. The powerful clerics rage at being named, but the pluralist tradition gains a defender and the killings slow.

right.label: Guard the shrines but spare the preachers.
right.tooltip: I will protect the shrine without naming the mosque.
right.reveal: You post guards but avoid confronting the inciting clerics. The attacks continue, fed by sermons you dare not touch, and the shrine-keepers learn that protection stops at the pulpit.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: Time has not healed it — the conflict comes back armed. A horrific gang-rape, filmed and shared by the attackers — politically connected young men — has ignited nationwide protests demanding justice and the death penalty. The IGP asks whether to let the case take its full course against the connected, or let it quietly stall.

left.label: Pursue the connected attackers fully.
left.tooltip: A crime this vile cannot buy its way free.
left.reveal: You let the case proceed hard against the well-connected accused. Their patrons lean on you and lose, the protesters are vindicated, and rare faith in justice flickers back to life.

right.label: Let the case quietly stall.
right.tooltip: Their families are friends I cannot afford to lose.
right.reveal: You let the case sink into delay and lost evidence. The connected walk, the protests curdle into cynicism, and every woman in the country reads the verdict on whose justice this is.

speaker: Border Guard Director
prompt: Once again, and with the whole house holding its breath. Your own border guards run a thriving smuggling trade — cattle, drugs, gold, phensedyl — in collusion with their counterparts across the wire. The director can purge the corrupt battalions, risking morale and a guarded border, or keep the lucrative status quo.

left.label: Purge the smuggling battalions.
left.tooltip: A border guard who smuggles guards nothing.
left.reveal: You root out the corrupt commanders and break the rackets. Morale dips and the border briefly wobbles, but the contraband flood eases and the force's discipline begins to mend.

right.label: Leave the border rackets running.
right.tooltip: A purge now leaves the wire unwatched.
right.reveal: You leave the smuggling economy intact to keep the battalions content. The border stays manned and the contraband — drugs especially — keeps pouring across into the towns.

speaker: Home Secretary
prompt: The matter resurfaces in a harder year, patience exhausted. Unrest is spreading city to city and the secretary urges a nationwide curfew with shoot-on-sight authority to 'restore order overnight'. It would clear the streets — and any death under it becomes a death you ordered.

left.label: Impose curfew with shoot-on-sight.
left.tooltip: Order restored at dawn is worth a hard night.
left.reveal: The curfew empties the streets and the unrest is frozen by force. The shoot-on-sight deaths become infamous, and the quiet you buy is the quiet of a country holding its breath in fear.

right.label: Impose a curfew without lethal force.
right.tooltip: A curfew is a pause, not a firing line.
right.reveal: You order a curfew but forbid shooting into crowds, relying on presence and arrests. The streets clear more slowly and unevenly, but the night does not end with a massacre to answer for.

speaker: Telecom Security Officer
prompt: A later season of rule, the stakes now openly existential. With protests organising by phone, the officer can order a total internet and mobile blackout to blind and scatter them. It would choke the coordination — and the economy, the hospitals, and the country's last window onto what your forces are doing in the dark.

left.label: Order a total communications blackout.
left.tooltip: Cut the signal and the crowd cannot gather.
left.reveal: The country goes dark and the protests fragment. Commerce freezes, hospitals scramble, and the blackout itself — and what it hides — becomes the global headline against you.

right.label: Keep the networks on, target specific cells.
right.tooltip: A nation blinded is a nation that assumes the worst.
right.reveal: You leave the internet up and pursue specific organisers instead. The protests stay coordinated, but the economy runs, the hospitals function, and your forces act in daylight.

speaker: Police Reform Commissioner
prompt: It comes around again, and the margin for error is gone. A reform drive proposes body cameras and a real complaints commission for the police — a check on the brutality that fuels public hatred of the force. The rank-and-file and their commanders see it as a leash and a humiliation, and resist hard.

left.label: Mandate body cameras and oversight.
left.tooltip: A camera on the badge is a witness for the citizen.
left.reveal: You force body cameras and an independent complaints body. The commanders bristle and the footage occasionally damns them, but public trust in the police, long dead, stirs faintly.

right.label: Drop the accountability reforms.
right.tooltip: A leashed force is a hesitant one when it matters.
right.reveal: You shelve the cameras and the commission to keep the force happy. The brutality goes on unrecorded, and the gulf between the police and the people they answer to yawns wider.

speaker: District Magistrate
prompt: The same demand, but the reign is older and the knives sharper. Self-appointed morality squads are harassing couples in parks, attacking 'immodest' women, and beating young people for un-Islamic dress. The hardliners call them guardians of virtue. The magistrate asks whether to arrest the squads or quietly tolerate them.

left.label: Arrest the morality squads.
left.tooltip: The state polices the streets, not a self-righteous mob.
left.reveal: You jail the vigilantes and protect the harassed. The hardline preachers howl about defending vice, but the parks and streets become a little freer and a little safer.

right.label: Tolerate the morality patrols.
right.tooltip: Let the pious keep the streets decent.
right.reveal: You look away as the squads enforce their code. The hardliners are gratified, and a creeping, unofficial morality police takes root, answering to no law but its own.

speaker: Intelligence Chief
prompt: Years deep, the unresolved returns to be resolved for good. Investigators have located a secret detention site — an 'Aynaghar' of mirrored cells where the disappeared are held off the books. The story is about to break. The chief asks whether to deny everything and bury the site, or acknowledge it and free the captives.

left.label: Deny it and bury the site.
left.tooltip: A secret admitted is a secret weaponised.
left.reveal: You raze the site, scrub the records, and deny it ever existed. The denials collapse under the survivors' testimony, and the secret prison becomes the defining symbol of your rule.

right.label: Acknowledge it and free the captives.
right.tooltip: A crime confessed can at least be ended.
right.reveal: You shut the site, release the held, and promise an inquiry. The admission is a bombshell and the security establishment panics, but the disappeared come home and a monstrous chapter closes.

speaker: Hill Tracts Security Officer
prompt: The conflict reignites, and the world is watching this time. In the hill tracts, the army's web of checkpoints and camps controls indigenous life and feeds a low-grade insurgency, while settlers and soldiers want the grip kept tight. The officer asks whether to draw down the military presence per the peace accord, or hold it.

left.label: Hold the heavy military grip.
left.tooltip: A drawdown invites the insurgents back.
left.reveal: You keep the camps and checkpoints in place. The settlers and brass are reassured, but indigenous resentment festers under the daily indignity, and the accord rots another year.

right.label: Draw down per the peace accord.
right.tooltip: A region held at gunpoint is never truly held.
right.reveal: You pull back camps and ease the checkpoints as the accord requires. The army and settlers warn of chaos, but the hill peoples breathe, and a buried peace process stirs.

speaker: District Magistrate
prompt: A familiar crisis, escalated by everything you let slide. A doctored screenshot alleging a Hindu youth insulted the Prophet has set mobs torching a minority village. The youth may well be innocent and framed. The magistrate asks whether to protect the village and investigate the framing, or appease the mob by arresting the accused.

left.label: Protect the village, investigate the framing.
left.tooltip: A village burned on a forgery is a crime against us all.
left.reveal: You deploy force to shield the village and pursue whoever forged the post. The mob's instigators are exposed and jailed, and the framed youth is spared a fate sealed by a lie.

right.label: Arrest the accused to calm the mob.
right.tooltip: An arrest now is cheaper than a riot tonight.
right.reveal: You jail the accused youth to placate the crowd, guilt unproven. The village still smoulders, the real forgers go free, and the law bends, once again, to whoever can summon a mob.

speaker: Protocol Security Chief
prompt: The fault line cracks wider; the decision can no longer wait. A huge share of the police is tied up guarding ministers, MPs, and their families, stripping protection from ordinary neighbourhoods. The chief can slash the VIP details and redeploy to the public, or keep the entourages your elite has come to expect.

left.label: Slash VIP security, redeploy to the public.
left.tooltip: A constable guarding a minister's nephew is stolen from a slum.
left.reveal: You cut the bloated VIP details and push police back into the neighbourhoods. The elite fume at their shrunken entourages, but ordinary streets see a uniform again.

right.label: Keep the VIP protection as it is.
right.tooltip: An exposed minister is an unstable government.
right.reveal: You leave the entourages intact. The elite feel safe and flattered, and the ordinary neighbourhoods keep waiting for a police presence that is parked outside someone's mansion.

speaker: Cyber Patrol Commander
prompt: Late in the reign, the old question demands a heavier answer. A new cyber-patrol unit can monitor social media around the clock and arrest citizens for 'anti-state' posts — memes, jokes, criticism. The commander wants a wide mandate; it would chill all online speech and fill the jails with teenagers over Facebook posts.

left.label: Give the cyber patrol a wide mandate.
left.tooltip: A nation's mood is made on its phones; watch them.
left.reveal: The unit trawls the feeds and the arrests for posts begin. Online criticism withers into silence, and the spectacle of teenagers jailed over memes draws global derision.

right.label: Restrict it to genuine threats only.
right.tooltip: A meme is not a crime, and a jail is not a comments section.
right.reveal: You confine the unit to real incitement and terror, not criticism. The dissent stays loud and irritating online, but the jails are not filled with children over Facebook posts.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: It returns once more — and this round may be the one remembered. A court has ordered a vast slum cleared from contested land, and the police must execute it. The IGP can carry out a brutal dawn eviction with bulldozers, or slow-walk it and push for relocation and resettlement first.

left.label: Carry out the dawn eviction by force.
left.tooltip: A court order is a court order.
left.reveal: Bulldozers and baton charges clear the slum at first light. The land is cleared and the optics are savage — families weeping over rubble — and the resentment will outlast the headlines.

right.label: Slow-walk it, push for resettlement first.
right.tooltip: I will not bulldoze the poor into the street at dawn.
right.reveal: You delay enforcement and press for relocation sites before any clearance. The landowner and the court grow impatient, but the eviction, when it comes, does not begin with families in the rain.

speaker: Auxiliary Forces Director
prompt: The reckoning circles back, grown teeth in the waiting. The vast village auxiliary force could be armed and empowered as a rural security backbone — cheap manpower loyal to the state — or it risks becoming a politicised village militia settling local scores under colour of authority. The director awaits your call.

left.label: Arm and empower the village auxiliaries.
left.tooltip: A loyal force in every village is a state in every village.
left.reveal: You arm and expand the auxiliaries into a rural security web. Coverage spreads cheaply, and so does the abuse, as local commanders use their new authority to settle old scores.

right.label: Keep the auxiliaries unarmed and limited.
right.tooltip: An armed militia in every village is a thousand small tyrannies.
right.reveal: You keep the force unarmed and tightly bounded. Rural security leans more on the regular police, but you avoid seeding the countryside with armed men answering to local bosses.

speaker: Financial Crimes Chief
prompt: Another term, the same antagonist, the stakes raised again. A sophisticated counterfeit-currency ring, run from across the border, is flooding the markets with fake notes and eroding trust in the cash economy. The chief can mount a costly crackdown and currency-upgrade, or let it run and quietly manage the damage.

left.label: Crack down and upgrade the currency.
left.tooltip: Fake money is a slow poison in the bloodstream.
left.reveal: You smash the distribution networks and roll out harder-to-forge notes. The operation is costly and disruptive at the tills, but trust in the cash in people's hands is restored.

right.label: Let it run and manage the damage.
right.tooltip: A currency overhaul is a budget I will not spend on a nuisance.
right.reveal: You leave the ring largely alone and absorb the losses. The fakes keep circulating, the cash economy frays at the edges, and the cross-border syndicate grows bolder.

speaker: Women's Safety Officer
prompt: The matter, long deferred, lands with the force of a verdict. Street harassment of girls and women — 'eve-teasing' that has driven some to suicide — is endemic and unpunished. The officer wants dedicated rapid-response units, mobile courts, and harassment made a real, enforced offence; the cultural pushback frames it as policing male nature.

left.label: Enforce harassment as a real crime.
left.tooltip: A girl afraid to walk to school is a society failing her.
left.reveal: You stand up rapid-response teams and mobile courts and start jailing harassers. The cultural grumbling is loud, but girls and women begin, cautiously, to reclaim the streets.

right.label: Leave it to social norms.
right.tooltip: The street cannot be policed for every catcall.
right.reveal: You decline to make it a priority. The harassment goes on unchecked, the streets stay hostile to half the population, and another schoolgirl's suicide note names names no one will pursue.

speaker: Counter-Terror Commander
prompt: Years on and unhealed, the conflict returns to claim its answer. Machete-wielding militants are hacking secular bloggers and publishers to death in the streets, and a hit-list circulates online. The commander wants the targets given protection and the cells hunted; hardliners hint the victims 'insulted faith' and deserve no sympathy.

left.label: Protect the targets and hunt the cells.
left.tooltip: A citizen's life is not forfeit for a sentence he wrote.
left.reveal: You assign protection to the listed and hunt the killers hard. The hardliners mutter that you defend blasphemers, but the murders slow and the state shows it shields even those it disagrees with.

right.label: Quietly let the matter lie.
right.tooltip: I will not spend the force defending men who provoke the pious.
right.reveal: You offer little protection and a soft pursuit. The hardliners are satisfied, the hit-list lengthens, and the world watches secular voices hacked down while the state looks away.

speaker: Police Reform Commissioner
prompt: The same fault line, the harder hour, the narrower path. Investigations here lean on confessions beaten out of suspects on remand, sending innocents to the gallows and letting real culprits walk. The commissioner wants forensic, evidence-based policing and an end to torture-remand; the old guard says it ties their hands.

left.label: End torture-remand, build forensic policing.
left.tooltip: A confession under torture convicts the innocent and frees the guilty.
left.reveal: You ban torture-remand and invest in forensics and training. The old guard complains their hands are tied, but wrongful convictions fall and real evidence starts to convict the real culprits.

right.label: Leave the remand culture intact.
right.tooltip: A force without remand is a force without leverage.
right.reveal: You leave the beat-a-confession culture untouched. Cases 'close' fast on coerced statements, innocents hang, and the real criminals — and the rot in the method — go on undisturbed.

speaker: Immigration Director
prompt: It comes back sharpened by every season you survived. The passport and immigration offices run on bribes — fast-tracking the payers, stalling the honest, and quietly issuing genuine documents to criminals and traffickers. The director can digitise and clean the system or keep the lucrative counters humming.

left.label: Digitise and clean the passport system.
left.tooltip: A passport sold to a trafficker is a border breached.
left.reveal: You automate and audit the offices and the bribery withers. Honest applicants get served and the document-for-criminals trade is choked, and the counter staff who lived on bribes seethe.

right.label: Leave the passport counters as they are.
right.tooltip: A demoralised office processes nothing.
right.reveal: You leave the racket running. The bribes flow and the queues move for those who pay, and genuine passports keep finding their way into the hands of traffickers and fugitives.

speaker: Law Secretary
prompt: A later year of power, the old crisis now a true test of it. A condemned man's mercy petition sits on your desk amid real doubts about his trial — a coerced confession, a recanted witness. The base bays for the hanging; a quiet voice says executing a possibly innocent man is a stain that never washes out.

left.label: Let the execution proceed.
left.tooltip: The court has spoken; mercy is weakness.
left.reveal: You reject the petition and the man hangs. The base is satisfied, and the unexamined doubts about his guilt harden into a likely miscarriage you can never undo.

right.label: Commute the sentence pending review.
right.tooltip: A doubt this size cannot end on a gallows.
right.reveal: You commute the sentence and order the doubts examined. The base howls about coddling criminals, but you refuse to make the state the executioner of a man it may have wronged.

speaker: Fire Service Director
prompt: The unresolved returns, and this time it will not be deferred. The capital's fire service cannot reach half its high-rises, its hydrants are dry, and its ladders are too short for the towers that keep burning with people trapped inside. The director needs a costly modernisation; the money is wanted elsewhere until the next blaze.

left.label: Fund the fire-service modernisation.
left.tooltip: A ladder too short is a coffin with a view.
left.reveal: You re-equip the fire service with tall ladders, working hydrants, and training. The cost is heavy and the danger feels abstract between fires, but the next tower blaze meets a service that can reach it.

right.label: Defer it until after the next disaster.
right.tooltip: The budget has louder claims than a quiet ladder.
right.reveal: You put the modernisation off. The money goes to noisier needs, and the next high-rise fire finds the same dry hydrants and short ladders, and the same trapped people at the windows.

speaker: Border Guard Director
prompt: A term later, the same fault line tears open again. Along the border, official 'haat' markets could legalise the petty cross-border trade that locals depend on and that smugglers now run. The director can expand the legal haats, undercutting the smuggling mafias, or keep the border sealed and the black trade thriving.

left.label: Expand the legal border markets.
left.tooltip: A legal stall starves an illegal smuggler.
left.reveal: You expand the border haats and the petty trade comes into the open. The smuggling mafias lose ground, border communities prosper, and tensions along the wire ease a little.

right.label: Keep the border sealed.
right.tooltip: An open market is an open door.
right.reveal: You keep the border tight and the haats few. Security looks robust on paper, and the smuggling mafias keep their monopoly on the trade the locals will pursue regardless.

speaker: Crime Investigation Chief
prompt: Years into your rule, the matter you thought buried resurfaces — harder. A wave of kidnappings-for-ransom is terrifying the business class, and some cases trace to off-duty police and political cadres. The chief can mount a real crackdown that reaches into your own ranks, or focus on the freelance gangs and spare the connected.

left.label: Crack down, your own ranks included.
left.tooltip: A kidnapper in uniform is the worst kind.
left.reveal: You pursue every thread, including the police and cadres involved. The business class is reassured and the connected are rattled and angry, but the kidnapping wave breaks.

right.label: Chase the freelancers, spare the connected.
right.tooltip: Some uniforms are better not investigated.
right.reveal: You round up the freelance gangs and leave the connected untouched. The headlines look tough, but the protected predators keep operating and the business class quietly moves its families abroad.

speaker: Rural Justice Officer
prompt: A new season of power, an old problem with sharper teeth. In the villages, informal arbitration councils — shalish — increasingly issue cruel, illegal punishments: lashings, hilla marriages, and worse, mostly against women, under the colour of religion. The officer wants them reined in by the formal courts; the rural clergy resist.

left.label: Rein in the shalish, enforce the law.
left.tooltip: A village court that lashes women is a crime, not a custom.
left.reveal: You move to subordinate the shalish to formal law and prosecute the cruellest verdicts. The rural clergy protest the intrusion, but women gain protection from punishments dressed as tradition.

right.label: Leave the village councils alone.
right.tooltip: I will not fight every village elder in the land.
right.reveal: You leave the shalish to their ways. The rural power-holders are placated, and the lashings, the hilla marriages, and the village 'justice' against women grind on beyond the law's reach.

speaker: Telecom Security Director
prompt: The crisis returns, and this time the cabinet is watching. A new law would let your agencies intercept any call or message — with, or without, a judge's warrant. The director wants the warrantless version for speed; civil society demands judicial oversight to stop the inevitable abuse against critics and rivals.

left.label: Pass warrantless interception powers.
left.tooltip: A warrant is a delay a plot does not grant me.
left.reveal: You take the warrantless powers. The agencies move fast on real threats and faster on inconvenient critics, and the surveillance state gains a legal blank cheque that will outlast you.

right.label: Require judicial warrants for interception.
right.tooltip: A wiretap with no judge is a weapon with no safety.
right.reveal: You insist on warrants and oversight. The agencies grumble about delay, but interception keeps a check against the day it would otherwise be turned wholesale on dissent.

speaker: Highway Police Chief
prompt: Once more the issue lands on your desk, the stakes higher than before. Armed gangs are robbing night buses and trucks on the highways, sometimes killing, and the long-haul transport that moves the economy is gripped by fear. The chief wants costly highway patrols and checkpoints; others say it is a local nuisance not worth the budget.

left.label: Fund highway patrols and checkpoints.
left.tooltip: A highway ruled by dacoits is an economy held hostage.
left.reveal: You blanket the highways with patrols and the robberies fall. The cost is real and the checkpoints slow some traffic, but the night roads grow safe enough to move goods and people again.

right.label: Treat it as a minor local matter.
right.tooltip: A few highway robberies do not justify a national force.
right.reveal: You leave the highways lightly policed. The budget is spared, the dacoit gangs grow bolder, and the long-haul transport that feeds the cities runs scared and dear after dark.

speaker: Social Crime Officer
prompt: Another year survived, and the reckoning circles back. Organised syndicates maim children and traffic the disabled into forced begging rings on the city streets. The officer wants the rings smashed and the victims rehabilitated — a costly, complex rescue — or you can keep clearing beggars off the streets for the optics.

left.label: Smash the rings, rehabilitate the victims.
left.tooltip: A child maimed for alms is a crime crying out.
left.reveal: You break the begging syndicates and build rehabilitation for the rescued. The work is hard and costly and the rings fight back, but maimed children are freed instead of just swept from view.

right.label: Just clear beggars off the streets.
right.tooltip: The tourists and the elite want clean pavements.
right.reveal: You mount street-clearing drives for the optics. The pavements look tidier for visitors, the syndicates simply relocate, and the trafficked children keep being maimed for someone else's alms.

speaker: Protocol Security Chief
prompt: The ground has shifted; the same demand arrives with new menace. Every ministerial movement shuts down the capital's arteries for VIP motorcades, trapping ambulances and commuters for hours in the world's worst gridlock. The chief can curb the closures and the convoys, or keep the elite gliding through a paralysed city.

left.label: Curb the VIP road closures.
left.tooltip: A minister's ten minutes is not worth a city's hour.
left.reveal: You slash the motorcade privileges and the blanket closures. The elite chafe at sharing the roads, but ambulances move, commuters get home, and the city sees its rulers join the traffic.

right.label: Keep the VIP convoys gliding.
right.tooltip: A minister stuck in traffic is a state diminished.
right.reveal: You keep the motorcade closures. The elite glide through on cleared roads, and the rest of the capital — ambulances and all — sits choking in the gridlock the convoys leave behind.

speaker: Crime Investigation Chief
prompt: History rhymes: the conflict reopens deeper than last time. A shoddily-built tower has collapsed, killing scores, and the trail leads to a developer who bribed the building inspectors — a developer who funds the party. The chief asks whether to prosecute him fully or let the inspectors take the fall alone.

left.label: Prosecute the developer fully.
left.tooltip: A builder who buries his buyers must answer for it.
left.reveal: You charge the developer himself, donor or not. The party loses a funder and the building lobby trembles, but the bereaved see real accountability and the inspectors are not the only scapegoats.

right.label: Let the inspectors take the fall.
right.tooltip: A donor toppled topples a wing of the party.
right.reveal: You confine the blame to a few inspectors and shield the developer. The party keeps its funder, the building lobby relaxes, and the next shoddy tower is already rising on a bribed certificate.

speaker: Cyber Crime Chief
prompt: Mid-reign, the old wound is torn afresh. Scam operations are draining citizens' mobile wallets and bank accounts through phishing and fake-prize calls, some run from organised centres with insider help from telecom and bank staff. The chief wants a real cyber-fraud taskforce; standing it up is costly and technical.

left.label: Build a real cyber-fraud taskforce.
left.tooltip: A scam centre is a thief that never sleeps.
left.reveal: You fund a proper cyber-fraud unit with bank and telecom cooperation. The scam centres are raided and the insider leaks plugged, and citizens' savings grow a little safer online.

right.label: Leave it to overstretched local police.
right.tooltip: Online fraud is a nuisance, not a priority.
right.reveal: You leave cyber-fraud to thinly-spread local stations that cannot trace it. The scam centres flourish, the insider leaks continue, and the public learns that money lost online is money gone.

speaker: Public Order Officer
prompt: The pressure you deferred has come due, larger now. Competing religious processions are due to cross the same route on the same day, a flashpoint that has sparked riots before. The officer can impose strict separate routes and timings — heavy-handed and resented by both — or trust the organisers and keep policing light.

left.label: Impose strict separate routes.
left.tooltip: Two flashpoints kept apart make no fire.
left.reveal: You enforce separate routes and tight timings. Both communities grumble at the heavy hand, but the processions pass without collision and the day ends without a riot to mourn.

right.label: Trust the organisers, police lightly.
right.tooltip: A festival smothered in police is no festival.
right.reveal: You keep policing light and trust the community leaders. The day may pass in peace and harmony — or one hot-headed clash may ignite the riot the light touch failed to prevent.

speaker: Transport Security Coordinator
prompt: A later term, and the quarrel has hardened into a test. Before Eid, millions cram onto overloaded buses, trains, and ferries to reach their villages, and every year the holiday rush kills hundreds in crashes and capsizes. The coordinator wants strict overloading and safety enforcement; the public wants cheap passage home at any cost.

left.label: Enforce holiday travel safety hard.
left.tooltip: A homecoming should not end in a hearse.
left.reveal: You enforce capacity limits and roadworthiness through the rush. Fares rise and the crush at the terminals worsens, but the holiday's grim death toll on the roads and rivers falls sharply.

right.label: Let the holiday rush flow cheaply.
right.tooltip: The poor must reach home for Eid, rules or no.
right.reveal: You ease enforcement so everyone can afford to travel. The buses and ferries overload as always, the homecoming proceeds cheap and chaotic, and the holiday claims its usual harvest of the dead.

speaker: Crime Investigation Chief
prompt: The country has changed, but this fault line has not — only worsened. Illegal firearms are awash in the country, fuelling political violence and crime. The chief proposes a no-questions surrender amnesty to mop them up — which also lets the guilty erase the weapons tying them to past crimes. The alternative is a hard, raid-based recovery drive.

left.label: Offer a no-questions arms amnesty.
left.tooltip: A gun surrendered is a gun that kills no one.
left.reveal: You run the amnesty and weapons pour in by the thousand. The streets grow a little safer, and a number of past crimes lose the evidence that might have solved them.

right.label: Mount a hard, raid-based recovery.
right.tooltip: An amnesty launders the gunman with his gun.
right.reveal: You hunt the illegal arms through raids and intelligence. Fewer weapons come in and the operation is dangerous and slow, but the guns recovered come with the cases attached to them.

speaker: Diplomatic Security Chief
prompt: It returns, as these things do, with the patience gone. After threats against foreigners, aid agencies and embassies demand heavy protection for their staff or they will pull out, taking their programmes and goodwill with them. The chief can lock the foreign community in a security bubble, or reassure with lighter, smarter measures.

left.label: Wrap foreigners in a heavy security bubble.
left.tooltip: A dead foreigner is a global headline I cannot afford.
left.reveal: You blanket the foreign community in visible heavy security. The agencies stay, but the fortress optics signal a dangerous country, and the bubble itself becomes a target and a symbol.

right.label: Reassure with lighter, smarter measures.
right.tooltip: A fortress says 'flee' louder than any threat.
right.reveal: You opt for intelligence-led, lighter protection and quiet reassurance. It is a calculated risk, but the agencies stay, the country looks governable rather than besieged, and normal life goes on.

speaker: Social Welfare Magistrate
prompt: Years on, the matter is no longer private; the press is already circling. Children in conflict with the law are thrown into adult jails and 'correction' centres rife with abuse, emerging hardened criminals. The magistrate wants a real juvenile-justice system of diversion and rehabilitation; the law-and-order voices want them treated as the threats they'll become.

left.label: Build a rehabilitative juvenile system.
left.tooltip: A child jailed with hardened men comes out a hardened man.
left.reveal: You build diversion, separate facilities, and rehabilitation for child offenders. The tough-on-crime crowd scoffs, but children get a path back instead of a finishing school for criminals.

right.label: Keep treating young offenders harshly.
right.tooltip: A young criminal is still a criminal.
right.reveal: You keep juveniles in the adult-adjacent system. It looks tough and costs little now, and each cohort of abused children graduates from the cells into the very crime the harshness was meant to stop.

speaker: Surveillance Technology Director
prompt: The same knot, retied tighter, lands before you once more. A fleet of surveillance drones could watch borders and crime hotspots — and hover over every protest and gathering, mapping the crowd. The director wants the full fleet with crowd-monitoring; civil society warns it is an eye in the sky over every dissenter.

left.label: Deploy the full surveillance drone fleet.
left.tooltip: An eye in the sky misses nothing.
left.reveal: You acquire the drone fleet with crowd-monitoring. Borders and crime hotspots are watched, and so is every protest, every gathering, every face that ever assembles against you.

right.label: Limit drones to borders and disasters.
right.tooltip: A drone over every march is a state over every shoulder.
right.reveal: You confine the drones to borders, disasters, and crime scenes, barring protest surveillance. You gain real operational reach without building an aerial panopticon over public dissent.

speaker: Health Crime Officer
prompt: Another monsoon, another return of an argument you cannot end. A ring is buying kidneys from desperate villagers — coerced and cheated — for wealthy transplant tourists, with crooked doctors and brokers in the chain. The officer wants the whole network smashed; some of the clinics involved are powerful and well-connected.

left.label: Smash the organ-trafficking network.
left.tooltip: A poor man's kidney is not a commodity.
left.reveal: You break the ring, clinics and brokers and all, however connected. The transplant-tourism money dries up and powerful doctors face the law, and the cheated villagers gain a defender.

right.label: Pursue only the village brokers.
right.tooltip: The clinics have patrons I cannot afford to cross.
right.reveal: You arrest the low-level brokers and spare the connected clinics. The visible end of the trade is disrupted, the lucrative clinical core survives, and the poor keep being harvested for parts.

speaker: Telecom Security Officer
prompt: Deep into the reign, the issue demands a final-sounding answer. Biometric SIM registration would tie every phone to a fingerprint and ID — curbing the anonymous numbers used in crime and terror, and building a perfect map of who holds every phone in the country. The officer wants it mandatory; privacy advocates are alarmed.

left.label: Mandate biometric SIM registration.
left.tooltip: An anonymous phone is a criminal's best friend.
left.reveal: You require fingerprint registration for every SIM. Anonymous crime-phones vanish, and the state acquires a fingerprint-linked map of every phone and, through it, every person who carries one.

right.label: Register SIMs without the biometrics.
right.tooltip: A fingerprint for a phone number is a price too high.
right.reveal: You require ID but bar the biometric linkage. You gain most of the accountability without building a national fingerprint-to-phone database ripe for abuse.

speaker: Internal Affairs Director
prompt: The reckoning you postponed has matured into a crisis. Deaths in police custody, always ruled 'heart attacks' or 'suicides', are mounting and the families are organising. The director can establish a genuinely independent inquiry mechanism, exposing the torture behind the deaths, or keep the in-house whitewash that protects the force.

left.label: Establish independent custodial-death inquiries.
left.tooltip: A death in a cell the state cannot explain is a death the state caused.
left.reveal: You set up independent inquiries with real powers. The force resists and a few careers end, but the era of automatic 'heart attack' verdicts cracks, and custody grows a little less lethal.

right.label: Keep the in-house inquiries.
right.tooltip: An independent inquiry indicts the whole force.
right.reveal: You keep custodial deaths investigated by the police themselves. The verdicts stay tidy and the force protected, and the families learn that a death in custody here has only one official cause: misfortune.

speaker: River Police Chief
prompt: A new chapter, the same antagonists, less room to manoeuvre. Armed pirates rule stretches of the great rivers and the bay, robbing and ransoming fishermen and cargo boats, sometimes with bribed police looking on. The chief wants a real river-police push; it means costly boats and confronting the officers on the take.

left.label: Fund a real anti-piracy river force.
left.tooltip: A river ruled by pirates is a river lost.
left.reveal: You fund fast boats and clean out the complicit officers. The pirate gangs are broken, the fishermen and cargo boats breathe, and the river patrols' own corruption is purged.

right.label: Leave the rivers lightly patrolled.
right.tooltip: A few river pirates do not justify a navy.
right.reveal: You leave the rivers thinly policed. The budget is saved, the pirate gangs and their police protectors thrive, and the fishermen pay their ransoms and their bribes as a cost of the water.

speaker: Immigration Director
prompt: The old fault line widens; this time there is no quiet option. You can quietly add critics, activists, and inconvenient businessmen to an airport exit-control list, trapping them in the country without charge or notice. The director calls it a clean, deniable leash; civil society calls it abduction by paperwork.

left.label: Bar the critics from leaving.
left.tooltip: A man who cannot flee can be reached.
left.reveal: You list the critics and they are turned back at the airport, bewildered. The leash is effective and deniable, until the pattern leaks and 'jailed within their own country' becomes the headline.

right.label: Keep the exit list to genuine fugitives.
right.tooltip: An exit ban on a critic is a confession of fear.
right.reveal: You confine the list to actual suspects with cases. The critics keep their freedom to come and go, and you are spared the spectacle of dissidents stranded at the departure gate.

speaker: Education Board Chairman
prompt: Time has not healed it — the conflict comes back armed. National exam papers leak online before every sitting, sold by a ring reaching into the printing presses and the boards. The chairman wants a hard crackdown and a digital question system; the leak ring is lucrative and protected, and a botched reform could throw the whole exam into chaos.

left.label: Crack down and digitise the exams.
left.tooltip: An exam sold in advance is a generation cheated.
left.reveal: You break the leak ring and move to secure digital question delivery. The protected racket fights back and one rollout stumbles, but the exams stop being an open auction and merit means something again.

right.label: Patch the system and hope.
right.tooltip: A radical overhaul could collapse the whole exam.
right.reveal: You make cosmetic fixes and leave the ring largely intact. The exams proceed, the papers keep leaking to those who pay, and the honest student keeps losing to the connected cheat.

speaker: Public Service Commission Liaison
prompt: Once again, and with the whole house holding its breath. Government job exams — the holy grail for millions of youth — are riddled with leaked papers, impersonators, and posts sold to the highest bidder. The liaison can mount a real anti-fraud overhaul, exposing connected beneficiaries, or keep the lucrative status quo.

left.label: Overhaul the recruitment exams.
left.tooltip: A job bought is a youth's dream stolen.
left.reveal: You secure the exams with biometrics and audits and void the fraudulent results. The connected who bought their posts are exposed and furious, but a generation sees that merit might finally count.

right.label: Leave the recruitment racket running.
right.tooltip: Too many of the bought belong to the party.
right.reveal: You leave the exams compromised. The posts keep going to the connected and the payers, and the honest, brilliant youth who studied for years learn what their merit is worth here.

speaker: Narcotics Control Officer
prompt: The matter resurfaces in a harder year, patience exhausted. Bootleg toxic liquor has killed dozens of the poor in a single district, and the trade — banned, so unregulated — is run by syndicates with police protection. The officer can crack down hard on the bootleggers, or push the harder path of regulating and taxing safe alcohol.

left.label: Crack down hard on the bootleggers.
left.tooltip: Poison sold as drink is murder by the bottle.
left.reveal: You raid the bootleg syndicates and their police protectors. The killer batches are seized, but prohibition stays, the trade regroups underground, and the next toxic batch is already brewing.

right.label: Regulate and tax safe alcohol.
right.tooltip: Banning a drink only hands it to poisoners.
right.reveal: You move toward regulating and taxing safe alcohol, draining the bootleggers' market. The pious are scandalised and the politics are hard, but the poor stop dying from methanol in a bottle.

speaker: Prisons Inspector-General
prompt: A later season of rule, the stakes now openly existential. The women's wings of the jails are overcrowded and abusive, holding women on petty or fabricated charges, some with their children, in degrading conditions. The IG wants reform — separate facilities, legal aid, release of the wrongly held — at real cost and effort.

left.label: Reform the women's prisons.
left.tooltip: A jail that degrades women degrades the state that runs it.
left.reveal: You fund better facilities, legal aid, and a review that frees the wrongly held. The cost and effort are real, but women and their children stop rotting in conditions no law intended.

right.label: Leave the women's wings as they are.
right.tooltip: The prisons have louder problems than comfort.
right.reveal: You leave the women's wings to their squalor. The budget is spared, and women on petty and fabricated charges keep languishing, their children growing up behind bars beside them.

speaker: Cyber Crime Chief
prompt: It comes around again, and the margin for error is gone. Foreign-run online betting and casino apps are hooking the young and draining money abroad through illegal channels, with local agents and some officials taking a cut. The chief can block and prosecute hard, or quietly tolerate a trade that greases many palms.

left.label: Block and prosecute the gambling networks.
left.tooltip: An app that bankrupts the young is a crime, not a game.
left.reveal: You block the apps, choke the payment channels, and prosecute the local agents. The trade is disrupted and the capital flight slowed, and the officials who took their cut go quiet and resentful.

right.label: Tolerate the gambling trade.
right.tooltip: A vice this profitable has many friends.
right.reveal: You leave the betting apps largely alone. The palms stay greased and the trade flourishes, and the young keep gambling their families' money into accounts somewhere overseas.

speaker: Forensic Services Director
prompt: The same demand, but the reign is older and the knives sharper. The country's one overwhelmed forensic lab has a years-long backlog, so DNA and ballistic evidence arrives long after trials end, and justice runs on confessions instead of science. The director needs heavy investment in labs and trained scientists; the payoff is slow and invisible.

left.label: Invest heavily in forensic capacity.
left.tooltip: Justice without science is justice by guesswork.
left.reveal: You fund new labs, equipment, and forensic scientists. The backlog shrinks over years, evidence starts reaching trials in time, and the reliance on beaten confessions slowly gives way to proof.

right.label: Leave the forensic system starved.
right.tooltip: Labs are a luxury; confessions are free.
right.reveal: You leave the single lab to drown in its backlog. The budget is spared, and the courts keep convicting on confession and guesswork while the science gathers dust in an evidence locker.

speaker: Police Procurement Chief
prompt: Years deep, the unresolved returns to be resolved for good. A foreign vendor offers a large package of riot-control gear — water cannon, rubber bullets, tear gas, sound cannons. The chief wants the full arsenal for crowd control; critics note that 'less-lethal' tools, in untrained hands, still blind and kill protesters.

left.label: Buy the full riot-control arsenal.
left.tooltip: A well-equipped force controls a crowd without a massacre.
left.reveal: You acquire the arsenal. Crowd control gets a notch less lethal in theory, and in untrained hands the rubber bullets and gas still maim and kill, just with deniability.

right.label: Buy modest gear with strict training.
right.tooltip: A weapon is only as humane as the hand that wields it.
right.reveal: You buy a modest kit and invest in restraint and training. The arsenal is smaller, but the officers learn to use it as a last resort, and crowd policing grows genuinely less deadly.

speaker: Women's Crime Officer
prompt: The conflict reignites, and the world is watching this time. A young bride has been killed over an unpaid dowry, and the in-laws — locally powerful — are buying off the police and the witnesses as such cases routinely vanish. The officer asks whether to drive the prosecution hard or let it fade like the others.

left.label: Drive the dowry-murder prosecution.
left.tooltip: A bride burned for money will have her day in court.
left.reveal: You protect the witnesses and push the case against the powerful in-laws. They lean on every connection and lose, and for once a dowry murder ends in a verdict instead of a settlement.

right.label: Let the case fade like the others.
right.tooltip: The in-laws have friends I would rather keep.
right.reveal: You let the case dissolve in bought witnesses and lost files. The powerful in-laws walk, the dead bride joins the uncounted, and every dowry-taker learns the price of a daughter-in-law's life.

speaker: Telecom Security Director
prompt: A familiar crisis, escalated by everything you let slide. Citizens are using VPNs to slip past your website blocks and reach banned news and platforms. The director wants VPNs themselves blocked and their users tracked, sealing the information bubble; the alternative is to accept that the net cannot be fully walled.

left.label: Block VPNs and track their users.
left.tooltip: A wall with a tunnel under it is no wall.
left.reveal: You move to block VPNs and flag their users. The information bubble tightens toward North-Korean completeness, the tech-savvy find new tunnels anyway, and the censorship draws global scorn.

right.label: Accept that the net cannot be sealed.
right.tooltip: A government at war with VPNs is a government afraid of news.
right.reveal: You stop chasing VPNs. The blocks stay porous and the banned sites reachable, but you are spared the futile, reputation-shredding crusade against the open internet.

speaker: Social Welfare Director
prompt: The fault line cracks wider; the decision can no longer wait. Tens of thousands of children live on the city streets — sniffing glue, prey to gangs, traffickers, and abusive police. The director wants a real network of shelters, schooling, and family reunification; the cheaper option is the periodic round-up that just moves them on.

left.label: Build shelters and reunification programmes.
left.tooltip: A child on the street is a future thrown away.
left.reveal: You build shelters, schooling, and family-tracing for street children. The cost is real and the work slow, but children are pulled off the streets into something like a childhood.

right.label: Stick with periodic round-ups.
right.tooltip: A shelter network is a budget I do not have.
right.reveal: You keep the cheap round-up drives that scoop children up and dump them elsewhere. The streets look briefly clearer, and the children drift back to the glue, the gangs, and the traffickers.

speaker: Cyber Crime Chief
prompt: Late in the reign, the old question demands a heavier answer. Intimate images — real and faked — are being weaponised to blackmail and shame women and girls, driving some to suicide, while victims who report are themselves blamed and exposed. The chief wants a dedicated, confidential unit; building it means resources and a cultural fight.

left.label: Build a confidential image-abuse unit.
left.tooltip: A girl shamed to death by a screen deserved a state that fought for her.
left.reveal: You stand up a confidential unit that pursues the blackmailers and protects the victims. The cultural pushback is real, but women begin to report without being destroyed for it.

right.label: Leave it to ordinary stations.
right.tooltip: These are private matters, not police priorities.
right.reveal: You leave image-abuse to the ordinary stations that blame and expose the victims. The blackmailers operate freely, and the suicides keep coming, each one a girl the state would not protect.

speaker: Public Order Officer
prompt: It returns once more — and this round may be the one remembered. A massive open-air concert and several large public events are planned for the same weekend, with crowd-safety planning that is, as ever, an afterthought. The officer wants proper crowd management and caps imposed; the organisers and the public resent the fences and limits.

left.label: Impose proper crowd-safety management.
left.tooltip: A joyous crowd can become a crush in a heartbeat.
left.reveal: You enforce capacity caps, exits, and crowd management. The organisers grumble at the cost and the public at the fences, but the weekend's celebrations end without a stampede to bury.

right.label: Let the events run loosely.
right.tooltip: Do not smother a celebration in barriers.
right.reveal: You keep the events open and lightly managed. The crowds flow free and vast and joyous, and the absence of a plan means one surge from turning a festival into a funeral.

speaker: Rural Justice Officer
prompt: The reckoning circles back, grown teeth in the waiting. In remote areas, people — usually poor women — are branded witches or sorcerers and beaten, exiled, or killed by mobs, often at a 'healer's' word. The officer wants awareness drives and hard prosecution; the local power structure shrugs it off as backward custom.

left.label: Prosecute and educate against the violence.
left.tooltip: A woman killed as a witch is a murder, not a custom.
left.reveal: You prosecute the mob leaders and run awareness drives with the rural clergy and schools. Change is slow against deep superstition, but the killings draw consequences and the accused gain protectors.

right.label: Dismiss it as backward custom.
right.tooltip: I cannot police every village's superstitions.
right.reveal: You leave the matter to the locals. The 'healers' keep naming witches, the mobs keep their impunity, and poor women keep being beaten and burned for crimes that do not exist.

speaker: NID Database Director
prompt: Another term, the same antagonist, the stakes raised again. Genuine national ID cards are being sold to non-citizens — including, the spies warn, to Rohingya and foreign criminals — through corrupt officials, eroding the integrity of the whole citizenship system. The director can purge and prosecute, or quietly manage the embarrassment.

left.label: Purge the fraud and prosecute officials.
left.tooltip: A citizenship sold is a nation cheapened.
left.reveal: You audit the database, cancel the fraudulent IDs, and jail the officials who sold them. The system's integrity is restored at the cost of a noisy scandal and some genuinely caught-up innocents.

right.label: Quietly manage the embarrassment.
right.tooltip: A public purge advertises how porous we are.
right.reveal: You handle it discreetly, cancelling a few cards and hushing the rest. The scandal stays small, and the corrupt officials and the trade in genuine IDs to non-citizens carry quietly on.

speaker: Registrar General
prompt: The matter, long deferred, lands with the force of a verdict. Marriage registrars routinely falsify brides' ages to legalise child marriages and forge documents for a fee, and the registry is a den of petty corruption. The registrar general wants digitised, age-verified registration; the registrars, a connected guild, will resist.

left.label: Digitise and age-verify marriage registration.
left.tooltip: A registrar who forges a child's age is an accomplice to her ruin.
left.reveal: You digitise registration with hard age verification. The registrars' guild howls at the lost fees, but child marriages lose their paper cover and the registry stops abetting them.

right.label: Leave the registrars to their trade.
right.tooltip: A connected guild is not lightly disturbed.
right.reveal: You leave the marriage registry as it is. The registrars keep their fees and their forgeries, and the falsified ages keep legalising the marriage of children across the country.

speaker: Narcotics Control Chief
prompt: Years on and unhealed, the conflict returns to claim its answer. Hundreds of thousands are addicted, and the few rehab centres are squalid, abusive, and out of reach of the poor. The chief wants a real, humane treatment network as the backbone of drug policy; the law-and-order voices still want jails and crossfire, not clinics.

left.label: Build a humane treatment network.
left.tooltip: An addict is a patient, not a body for the morgue.
left.reveal: You build accessible, humane rehabilitation as the spine of drug policy. The tough-on-drugs crowd scoffs, but families get their children back from addiction instead of from the river.

right.label: Stick with jails and crackdowns.
right.tooltip: Treatment coddles the weak; fear deters the rest.
right.reveal: You keep drug policy punitive. The squalid centres stay squalid and the addicted cycle through cells and crossfire, and the addiction the crackdowns were meant to crush keeps spreading.
